blocks.title: README.txt
blocks.content:
LIST OF APPS:

    - redrat_scripter: red rat code for infrared transceiver, usb, perl client that reads scripts and sends commands to server that uses sockets
    - redrat_blaster: used in server farm to blast with random IR messages to discover software errors
    - USB Device Driver: Modified to minimize memory usage and avoid excess of object recreation, objects were saved in an xml database and read as needed, used by redrat apps above.
    
    - ScreenCapture Control: ActiveX control to capture screens using various devices, creates images
    - Image Processing Code: binarize images, crop selected window
    - ScreenReader: Perform OCR on selected window
    - ScreenScraper: Perform OCR on a whole screen
    - AbbyReader OCR Instrumentation System, used by Server
    - OCR Server: Uses Abby Reader System, receives an image as input, performs OCR on it and returns text to the client, over TCP/IP Network.
    - OCR Server Tester: Performs various tests on the server.
    - OCR Client: Takes video screen shots with ScreenCapture, does some image processing, including adapted FFT algorithm, binarizes image, sends image to server, receives text, client is instrumented via javascript, usually from Test Director Web App.
    - OCR Client Blaster: Performance tester for OCR System.

LIST OF TECHNOLOGIES:

    - C/C++
    - JavaScript
    - Perl
    - ACE Framework
    - ActiveX
    - COM
    - Win32
    - USB Device Driver Development
    - OCR
    - Machine Vision
    - Image Processing
    - FFT, Fast (Discrete) Fourier Transform
    - Heuristics Methods for Machine Vision
    - Image Database Retreival System using above and FFT
    - Image Binarization
    - Image Capture
    - Embedded
    - Web Based Test: Test Director

WORK PERFORMED:

    - Envisioned, promoted, architected, designed and developed software infrastructure for Automation system in C++, ActiveX, COM and Win32. The Client/Server system uses ACE Framework design and architecture patterns and drives video capture devices to acquire and process images, perform OCR and recognition of other artifacts using the Fast Fourier Transform algorithm and other methods in Machine Vision. 
    - Invented algorithms using kernel methods and decision trees for analysis of image data and comparison for recognition. This was done in an incredible short time working independently, this took about one month of research and implementation.
    - Envisioned, promoted, designed and developed Linux Socket C++ Server and Perl Client script processing Automation application for driving libusb based RedRat infrared Transceiver concurrent multiple devices. A RedRat is a device that can read and replicate remote control signals. ANSI C, C++, OOP, Patterns, Image Processing, JavaScript, Test Director, Visual Studio, DirectX, USB Devices.
    - Applications and ActiveX controls were used from Test Directors and instrumented using javascript.

blocks.title: README
blocks.content:
DirectTV redrat automation system for linux (and perhaps windows too)

Authors: Original RedRat code by John Schmerge, also other authors of libusb
Author: Kevin Guerra, modified code and added many features including client/server code,
 added case insensitivity to dtvrrd.c/h. 
Turned into client/server app. Wrote Perl script dtvrrc to do the actual processing.

I use this program to perform tests on the HR20s and it allows me to test two boxes for comparison and regresion test. It saves time and makes it more fun to test. Basically the first time i setup a test i would create a new script file and enter the commands there manually while I press them with the remote and follow the leads. Then to run the same test again I just run the script. 

Thanks,
Kevin Guerra

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

NOTE: You do not need to know or understand all of this information to use this software, some technical details are
provided for completeness sake and for users that would like to know more. 

By default if the client detects that the server is not running it will run it and later shut it down, so you don't even need to start the server. But keep in mind that starting the server is the best way to use it. In fact, you do not even need to know your redrat(s) serial number(s).

To test this and to make sure everything is ok run the following after the installation:

'echo channel 7 sleep 5 info sleep 3 exit | dtvrrc' [Enter]

If everything went well you should see all connected redrats cause their attached stbs to change to channel 7, sleep 6 seconds, access the info screen, wait 4 seconds and then exit the info screen. Hey, wait a minute, why 6 seconds and 4 seconds, instead of 5 and 3. Well, the default frequency is 1000 miliseconds, which will be added to every command that interfaces with the stb. So channel 7 causes the client to wait 1000 ms or 1 extra second and then sleep 5 will wait 5 seconds for a total of 6.

WHAT IT IS:
This is a system to interpret custom scripts that it will use to send commands to redrat devices and execute other programatic 
operations. It is a system to aid in testing automation.

HOW IT WORKS:
The server dtvrrd will run in the background and will listen for connections from a client dtvrrc. The client will read
commands and text from your scripts or commands can be piped directly and will interpret them and execute or send them to the server accordingly.

SMALL EXAMPLES:
0) run rrs to make sure the dtvrrd server is running
1) from the command line type 'echo info sleep 2 exit |dtvrrc'
2) create a text file called myscript with the text 'setredrat CD1 frequency 1300 channel 7 loop 10 channel+ sleep 8 info sleep 3 exit endl' and run that script as follows 'dtvrrc myscript'

ADVANCED USAGE:
You can make your scripts executable as follows:
1) Create your script file and add commands.
2) In the first line of your script add this line: '#!/usr/bin/perl /path/to/dtvrrc'
3) Make your script executable: chmod +x myscript
4) Run your script as: ./myscript

INSTALLATION:
run ./install.sh
that will copy your files to the user ~/bin directory and will modify your ~/.bashrc file. 
after installation run source ~/.bashrc

CONFIGURATION:
1) If the client and server will run on the same machine, the defaults are fine and you can start writing scripts and running them without further ado.
2) Make sure there aren't any applications using port 7070 otherwise change the default as follows: run the server as 'env DTVRR_PORT=7071 sudo dtvrrd' and change that value in dtvrrc.cfg to use that same 7071 port.
3) Review the file dtvrrc.cfg to make sure the defaults fit your needs.
4) Add any macros you may need. Note: In future versions, the macro file should contain some useful macros.
5) You can run a script from another computer that connects to the server computer by changing the DTVRR_HOST variable to contain either the server name or its ip adress in the dtvrrc.cfg file. You could even run the dtvrrc client in a windows machine with cygwin and perl installed. 

FEATURES:
Server
1) Client/Server operation. This means you can run several scripts at the same time, even from different machines.
2) Configurable connection port and connection timeout, through the environment DTVRR_PORT(7070) and DTVRR_TIMEOUT(300 secs)
3) Redrat selection commands and keepalive command.
4) Internal usb/redrat device mapping. Fixes problems when the user disconnects a redrat and later reconnects. The server addresses the redrats by serial number initially but keeps an internal list since all internal addressing is by index, for performance reasons.
5) Automatic disconnection of clients based on timeout.
6) Logging to /tmp/dtvrrd.log for errors and connection activity and to /tmp/dtvrrd.msg for client messages, max size is 10 megs
7) Automatic detection of connection/disconnection of redrat devices.
8) The server recognizes each client by its connected socket number which usually starts at number seven. The client does not "know" this number, but you can view it in the /tmp/dtvrrd.log file for debugging purposes.
9) The server keeps a list of redrats assigned to each client, and will free them automatically when a client disconnects.
10) When the server is first run and when a redrat is connected or disconnected the server will output a list of ALL redrats to the log file. Keep this in mind because the list will contain the serial numbers, in case you didn't know your device SN.
11) Hot/Cold plug capability. I don't really know which one is which, but what this means is that you can unplug a redrat and later plug it back in, and your script should continue to run and the redrat should again respond to the commands. However, be nice and don't use this feature, because it fails on rare ocassions. So if you disconnect a redrat while running your script, restart the server and your script.
12) Server command status response to client
13) Command line switches -p port -t time out -d xmldb file

Client
1) Include files, macros, command line parameters and its own configuration file which are all optional and will use defaults.
2) Configurable parameters with defaults. They can be overridden through the environment or dtvrrc.cfg file.
	"DTVRR_HOST"		=> "localhost",		computer with dtvrrd running
	"DTVRR_PORT"		=> 7070,		port that dtvrrd listens on
	"DTVRR_FREQUENCY" 	=> 1000,		milliseconds between each command sent to a redrat
	"DTVRR_TIMEOUT"		=> 300,			timeout in seconds for client disconnections
	"DTVRR_DELAY"		=> 0,			extra delay after "certain" commands
	"DTVRR_REDRATSN"      	=> "*", 		default redrat to send commands to, * means use all available redrats
	"DTVRR_SERVEREXE"	=> "dtvrrd",		name of server application, this is used to check if it's running, if it
							is not, it will be started and shutdown at the end of the script.
	"DTVRR_SERVERMODE	=> "0",			0 = manual server start; 1 = auto start/shutdown;
							2 = auto start, no shutdown
3) Redrat selection commands and keepalive command.
4) Commands are output as they are executed and when nesting loops the tabbing is changed to reflect that fact, and to give the user better feedback and more readability.
5) Should be 100% compatible with the redrat software for windows scripts, including the loop command, for which you can use endl or loop for loop termination. However if you use the keyword loop for loop termination it *must* be the only command in that line.

FILES:

MG32081.xml	commands database, it contains all commands in the remote control for HR20's model number MG32801
dtvrrd.xml	same as above. This is the file that the server app looks for in the directory where the executable is located
dtvrrtrn	add commands to XML database, you shouldn't need to run this.
dtvrrd		main application, it is a daemon (system service) which receives tcpip connections and sends commands to redrat
dtvrrc		client application, perl script reads in script text files with commands to send to redrat
rrs		helper script to startup the system: sudo pkill dtvrrd ; sudo dtvrrd

BASIC USAGE:
1) Start 'dtvrrd' process.		sudo dtvrrd (or type rrs as a shortcut)
2) pipe script file to 'dtvrrc'		cat myscript | dtvrrc

DETAILED USAGE and SCRIPT SYNTAX: (what's in your script...)
0) You can put the commands in the same line in separate lines, use comment lines or comments after the commands.
NOTE: exception to this rule, commands print and dialog should be on one line.
1) All commands from the remote MG32081, according to their name, color or function, for details view commands.txt
2) Commands are CASE INSENSITIVE, so you can use Channel or channel and not have to worry about it.
3) List of script directives (also commands, in your script):
	COMMAND		SYNTHAX			EXAMPLE			EXPLANATION
	 ) comments	; or #			#this is a comment 
	a) DELAY	delay miliseconds	delay 2000		wait two seconds
	b) SLEEP	sleep seconds		sleep 2			wait two seconds
	c) PRINT/OUTPUT	print/output word	print hello world	it just prints text (keep command and text on same line)
	d) WAIT		wait 			wait			waits indefinitely until user inputs a key
	e) DIALOG	dialog text		dialog hello you	(keep command and text on same line)
	f) FREQUENCY	frequency ms		frequency 1000		interval between commands
	g) CHANNEL	channel number		channel 70		it's just like doing '7 0 enter' pick the synthax you like
	h) LOOP		loop number cmds endl   loop 3 channel+ endl    
	i) KEEPALIVE	keepalive		keepalive		make sure script does not timeout 
	j) KEYPAD	keypad ~text		keypad hanks_tom	Ok, this is my favorite command. Do you remember the keypad
		in the directv ui? For example when you do a search you have to enter some text. How do you script that? Well, you 
		can figure out how many down, up, left, right, select, etc... keys you need to enter. This will work ok, but in your
		script, it looks really messy and very verbose. Solution, use the keypad command, duh! This command will just figure
		out all the keys it needs to enter for you. the ~ (tilde) character will cause keypad to clear any text first, and 
		the _ (underscore) character *is* the space.
	k) TIMEOUT	timeout minutes		timeout 10		causes the script to exit after the number of minutes
	l) REDRAT SELECTION COMMANDS These commands work on a client by client or script by script basis. Meaning whatever command
			you use will apply to the script for which it is called. When a client script is run dtvrrc assigns by
			default all available redrats, this is not nice. So make sure your first line reads addredrat SN or
			setredrat SN. Ok the reason it was done this way is so that people that don't read manuals or info pages
			are able to use this system anyway. Which in most cases will be one computer, one user, one script. These
			commands are otherwise important if the user needs/wants/??? to run two or more scripts concurrently.
		selectallredrats	try to use all available (not used by other script/client) redrats
		deselectallredrats	give back redrats that the script is using.
		addredrat SN 		try to use redrat with serial number SN 
		delredrat SN		give back redrat with serial number SN
		setredrat SN		shorthand for deselectallredrats AND addredrat SN
4) dtvrrc.cfg and dtvrrc.macros are searched in this order: 
	a) current directory
	b) directory where dtvrrc script is located
	c) /etc/ directory
5) To include another script file in your script use something like '@include /path/to/script.file'
6) To use a macro, the macro must be first defined in dtvrrc.macros, for example 'macro1=info sleep 2 exit' to use that macro in your script call it as @macro1
7) Command line parameters can be used to substitute anything that you want to change in your script externally, instead of editing your script everytime. For example, suppose you have a script with a loop that you want to run sometimes 10 times and at other times 1 million times, for stress testing. This can be accomplished as follows: 'loop @0 channel+ sleep 5 info sleep 2 exit endl' save that text to a file and call it as, for instance: 'dtvrrc myscript 10' this will cause the loop to happen 10 times. Notice that the numbering starts at @0 (zero) this is important, and if the number of parameters passed to the script is insufficient there will be trouble.

PROGRAM OPERATION:
1) When you do 'cat ????(file) | dtvrrc' or 'dtvrrc script.txt' the script reads in the whole file and strips comments and any white space
2) The file is parsed line by line and any include directives are processed then recursively, so that if one is found, it will start with the include and once that's done it will return where it left off. Macros are also converted at this stage as are command line parameters.
3) Script ends up as a long string and is converted into an array, the array is traversed and the text is parsed and interpreted as commands or other directives to the script.
4) dtvrrc enters a main loop which uses that array and the commands are sent to the server 'dtvrrd' through a socket connection. Or directives are interpreted and code executed or it may enter other loops.
5) The server logs all activity in /tmp/dtvrrd.log
6) dtvrrc will show each command as it is executed
7) if your script does not send a command within the set timeout, your script is terminated. You can use keepalive to prevent this.

TODO:
1) Sometimes, on rare ocassions if the user disconnects a redrat and later reconnects it, the device will appear unavailable.

SUGGESTIONS:
If you think of something useful that could be added, don't hesitate to share that information if it will make this app better.

CURRENT KNOWN ISSUES:
1) max red rats is 20, which is hardcoded, it could be expanded if necessary. This keeps memory usage low, which is a good thing.
2) max connections is 20, for the same reasons as above. This means you can only have 5 scripts running at any one time.
3) commands are queued by libusb, duri

… [truncated for display]

blocks.title: CaptureImage.cpp
proto.txt
blocks.content:
Hi,

Here is the update to the OCR client/server protocol, per our conversation at the meeting.

NOTE: It changed somewhat due to most (all?) structure members became required, so the types are implicit from their respective positions.

Please feel free to submit any question or suggestions.

Thanks,

##########################################################

Communication between a tcp/ip client/server ocr system

1) The server consumes bitmaps and this data is sent in one packet along with other relevant information.
2) The client sends the server said relevant information and bitmap data, and expects to receive text data along with confidence level information and color information. Or, upon failure, and ascii zero terminated string containing error information.
3) The server should be able to detect text and optionally upon request, text attributes such as bold or italic type font. 
4) The text returned, each character is two bytes in length, Unicode, because a) that's how it's returned, b) the ocr engine sometimes returns special unicode characters to signal certain conditions and c) icon recognition will use special non-ascii characters.
5) Icon recognition can be done in this version.

MagicNumber 4 bytes 0xABB1

MessageTypes 1 byte ENUM {
	0 = RequestPlainText,
	1 = ReturnPlainText,
	2 = RequestText,
	3 = ReturnText,
}

The following is an enumeration that describes "protocol data types," each is one byte long.
ProtoDataTypes 1 byte ENUM { 
	0 = RecognitionFlags, 
	1 = CharacterPropertiesFlag, 
	2 = Languages, 
}

RecognitionFlags 1 byte ENUM { BackgroundColor, Hyphenation } 
	(Optional, default=0x03 )
CharacterPropertiesFlag  1 byte ENUM { IsBold,IsItalic,IsSmallCaps,IsStrikeout,IsSubscript,IsSuperscript,IsUnderlined,IsSuspicious } 
	(Optional, default=0xFF [detect everything]) 
Languages 1 byte ENUM { English, Spanish } 
	(Optional, default=english 0x00)

WIDTH 		2 bytes (Required)
HEIGHT 		2 bytes (Required)
BPP		1 byte  (Required)

ARRAYLENGTH	2 bytes	(Required)

arrays {
	TEXT 		2 bytes 	(Unicode)
	CONFIDENCE 	1 byte 		(0-99% and 255=undefined)
	COLOR 		4 bytes 	(RGB)
	FONTSIZE 	4 bytes 	(In Tweeps)
	CHARACTERHEIGHT 4 bytes 	(In Pixels)
}

MESSAGES (NOTE: All structure members are required unless otherwise noted)

client -> server
{
	MagicWord: 		(int4)
	MessageDataLength: 	(int4) 
	MessageType: 		(int1) (TYPES 0 or 2)
	Width: 			(int2) 
	Height: 		(int2) 
	BPP: 			(int1) 
	RecognitionFlags:	(int1)
	CharacterPropertiesFlag:(int1) Note that here, these are ALSO recognition flags, and they are returned in the respective array as props
	Language		(int1)
	Bitmap Data,		(variable)
}

server -> client 
{
	MagicWord: 		(int4)
	MessageDataLength: 	(int4) 
	MessageType: 		(int1) TYPE 1
	Result			(int2)
	BackgroundColor, 	(int4) 
	ArrayLength, 		(int2) 
	Text Array,		(variable)
	Confidence Array,	(variable)
}

server -> client 
{
	MagicWord: 		(int4)
	MessageDataLength: 	(int4) 
	MessageType: 		(int1) TYPE 3
	Result			(int2)
	BackgroundColor, 	(int4) 
	ArrayLength, 		(int2) 
	Text Array,		(variable)
	Confidence Array,	(variable)

	CharacterPropertiesFlag Array,(variable)
	Color Array,		(variable)
	CharacterHeight Array,	(variable) This one can be used for validation
	FontSize Array,		(variable) as can this one
}

TODO (Possibly in the future, if necessary):
-----------------
form recognition (for each screen)
check mark recognition

blocks.content:
- For the future.. 
 - Support of signal types beyond RR3ModulatedSignal and DoubleSignal.
 - Update RR3 module api to expose new fns to send other signal types, 
   unsure yet re toggledata processing, where to store lastsent state for doublesignals etc
 - Increased code seperation
   Currently signaldb processing code is leaking out into client code of the signaldb, 
   ie iterators of signals, creation of new signal data structures etc... 
   Aim to define RR3 .c/.h to be a module that is soley concerned with retrieving/sending ir signals, 
   the signals to be defined at the IRPacket level, and blank templates to be 
   retrieved from the signaldb, and then populated with the ir data, and passed back to the client.
   Need to isolate all code from the internals of the data representation, as current 
   parser might need some extra space to store stuff later.
 - Better support for creation of AVDevices / adding signals to pre-existing AVDevices
 - Look more into lirc, to see if integration of rr3 as a lirc driver will be possible

	VendorName varchar (100) NULL ,
	ClientName varchar (100) NULL ,
	DeliverToName varchar (100) NULL ,
	POType varchar (25) NULL ,
	EntryDate smalldatetime NULL ,
	DueDate smalldatetime NULL ,
	ExpirationDate smalldatetime NULL ,
	OrigNo1 varchar (10) NULL ,
	DeptNo1 varchar (10) NULL ,
	OrigNo2 varchar (10) NULL ,
	DeptNo2 varchar (10) NULL ,
	OrigNo3 varchar (10) NULL ,
	DeptNo3 varchar (10) NULL 
)
GO

GRANT  SELECT  ON tblPurchReqInput  TO PurchReq
GO

/****** Object:  View dbo.qryPurchReqDetail    Script Date: 12/3/1998 12:58:10 PM ******/
CREATE VIEW qryPurchReqDetail AS

SELECT
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.BuyerName,
	CONVERT(char(10), PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.EntryDate, 101) AS 'EntryDate',
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.ReqNo,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.ClientName,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.DeliverToName,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.OrigNo1,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.DeptNo1,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.OrigNo2,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.DeptNo2,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.OrigNo3,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.DeptNo3,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.VendorName,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.PONo,
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.POType,
	CONVERT(char(10), PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.ExpirationDate, 101) AS 'ExpirationDate',
	CONVERT(char(10), PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.DueDate, 101) AS 'DueDate'

FROM PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq
GO

GRANT  SELECT  ON dbo.qryPurchReqDetail  TO PurchReq
GO

/****** Object:  View dbo.qryPurchReqResult    Script Date: 12/3/1998 12:58:10 PM ******/
CREATE VIEW qryPurchReqResult AS

SELECT
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.ReqNo, 
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.PONo, 
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.VendorName, 
	PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.ClientName, 
	CONVERT(char(10), PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq.DueDate, 101) AS 'DueDate'

FROM PurchReqAdmin.tblPurchReq
GO

GRANT  SELECT  ON dbo.qryPurchReqResult  TO PurchReq
GO

/****** Object:  Stored Procedure dbo.sp_UpdateTables    Script Date: 12/3/1998 12:58:10 PM ******/
CREATE PROCEDURE sp_UpdateTables AS

DROP INDEX tblPurchReq.idxPurchReqPONo
DROP INDEX tblPurchReq.idxPurchReq

TRUNCATE TABLE tblPurchReq

EXECUTE sp_rename tblPurchReq, tblPurchReqTemp
EXECUTE sp_rename tblPurchReqInput, tblPurchReq
EXECUTE sp_rename tblPurchReqTemp, tblPurchReqInput

CREATE UNIQUE CLUSTERED INDEX idxPurchReqPONo
	ON tblPurchReq (PONo)

CREATE INDEX idxPurchReq
	ON tblPurchReq (VendorName, ClientName)
GO

GRANT  EXECUTE  ON dbo.sp_UpdateTables  TO PurchReqAdmin
GO

#

7

Deployer
is a 32bit Windows appliation. It is used to copy files and
directoriesfrom a source server to a destination server.

#

8

$

9

K

10

Deployer
Features

FEATURES:

0._ 
  Multimple deployments. This is done in a batch mode, one
deployment after another, in series.

0._ Test mode. Allows you
to find any potential problems before an actual deployment.

0._
Pause and Un-Pause commands. Allow you to interrupt a deployment in
progress to perform other tasks in the workstation. This may be
necessary because a deployment in progress takes up many system
resources.

0._ Simultaneous multiprocess. Allows software
deployment to several locations at the same time. You can limit the
number of simultaneous processes running.

0._ Partial
deployments. You can deploy an application in as many steps as
necessary.

0._ Deployer creates destination paths. If you deploy
to a destination and it does not have the path the deployer will
attempt to create the path (given that you have appropriate rights to
the server).

0._ File login of messages and errors. Messages and
errors are logged in a file and in the database.

0._ Log
history. Deployer messages and error from previous deployments are
stored in a separate log file. This allows optimum efficiency in
database operations as history log can be quite large.

0._
Database storage of records and logs.

0._ Debug mode. Will
display message boxes for just about all program operations.

0._
Show processes as windows. This allows you to see what the running
processes are doing. It is recommended that this feature be disabled
so the processes will use less memory for maximumefficiency.

0._
Time zone aware. Time zone information along with servers shut down
and boot up times allow the Deployer to postpone a deployment to a
destination if either the source or destination servers are down.

0._
Own INI file. Most program settings are stored in Deployer.ini

0._
Database backup and compaction. If Deployer becomes rather slow in
database operations. It is recommended to perform a compact database
operation.

0._ New record defaults. Defaults for Source and
Destination records. This is useful when adding new records, so you
type less. The defaults can be changed in Deployer.ini

0._
Standard controls and visual aids. Tool bar, status bar, menu bar,
source and destinations grids, some commands are available with a
right mouse click, current database field automatic highlight and
color indicators.

LIMITATIONS:

0._ Works only
with Micorsoft and Novell networks.

0._ It may not always
disconnect all servers when deployment is done.

0._ The OCX
control Session from Novell upon failiure to connect displays a
dialog box. Causing the deployment to stop. This is a problem when
doing unattended deployments. To minimize risk of encountering this
problem use the test feature.

0._

If there were any errors you will be prompted with         
a message.

abruptly bya server shutting down.

kill all running processes when youcancel a deployment.

If
unchecked it will leave all running processes active until they
finish.

blocks.title: README.txt
blocks.content:
LIST OF APPS:

    - CommLib: DLL, rewrote winsock to handle double buffering for better performance, and because of some bugs
    - MonitorDLL: DLL to monitor computers, servers or other devices through CommLib Winsock TCP/IP
    - LiquidAudio streaming control: I designed it as an ActiveX control, it was used to stream audio
    - SCAN Application and related tools
    - ESCAN: 2nd version of the Scheduler. The scheduler allows one to visually script and schedule devices to operate locally or remote, including sending commands through email.
    - SiteLinx: connect two ESCAN servers through email, and send/execute scheduler commands for remote control
    - PC_Monitor: Win32 app to monitor a PC, it interfaced with the scheduler so as to report events
    - PC_Server: Win32 app to monitor a Video Server, it interfaced with the scheduler so as to report events
    - DeviceSimulator: Create devices to testing or for simulation of absent devices so that the scheduler can operate prior to installation at sites
    - XML ActiveX control: used to import data from a database and export to xml files. Very early one of the first xml systems.
    - Email ActiveX control: used to send/read email
    
    - Custom Applications: Developed for various clients, such as Voting System for the SHOA Foundation, Event generation devices for AMNH, etc...

LIST OF TECHNOLOGIES:

    - C/C++
    - TCP/IP
    - Win32
    - WinDbg
    - WinSock, Developed own version with DLL due to a bug
    - DDK, Device Driver Development
    - COM+
    - ActiveX
    - Windows Applications
    - DLL Development
    - Multi-Threading
    - XML 
    - MAPI, Mail API
    - TAPI, Telephony API
    - RAS, Remote Access Service
    - MPEG encoding/decoding
    - TV Closed Captioning
    - TV Output
    - GUI UX/UI Heavy Development
    - Printing
    - Embedded
    - Robotics
    - Video/Audio Streaming
    - Databases
    - Scheduling
    - Even Driven Programming
    - Remote Control through Email

WORK PERFORMED:

    - Designed, coded, tested, profiled and documented applications and tools and proposed changes and additions. Responsible for full development cycle. Worked in a team environments. Served as mentor to other programmers and technicians.
    - Conceived, designed and developed application for remote control and monitoring using C++, COM+, MAPI, TAPI, RAS.
    - Improved serial port and internet connectivity DLL which provided support for most applications. Developed second version, maximized capacity and transfer speed using C++, raw sockets, multi-threading, network events and double buffering.
    - Took ownership of application that controls and interacts with network and embedded devices. Developed protocols, device interfaces, ActiveX and ATL controls; extended application's and device's capabilities. Developed second version with many enhancements: MDI GUI, scripting capabilities, XML format, device triggers, email reporting plus many others. Used Visual C++, WinDbg and DDK.
    - Developed applications, tools and controls to support projects: Database, printing, MPEG encoding, hardware programming, device driver interface for video hardware to output graphics and text to TV screens, magnetic cards scanning, touch panels, Real Audio streaming control and device.
    - Adapted and implemented systems and applications for: American Museum of Natural History in New York, Museum of Tolerance in Beverly Hills, The Country Music Hall of Fame in Nashville, The Shoa Foundation in Universal Studios and others.

1Introduction

Easy
Schedule is a Windows 95/NT based time of day Audio Visual control
and monitoring system designed for continuous operation without the
necessity for operator intervention.  This manual provides a brief
introduction and tutorial for using and modifying the system.

There
are three essential components to the system, devices, sequences and
schedules. These are introduced in the sections below, and more fully
explained in later sections.

1.1Devices

A
device is a piece of equipment that can be controlled by the
scheduler. A typical example would be the Electrosonic Videoserver,
or an Alcorn McBride V16 show controller.  The scheduler contains
built in protocols for a number of devices, and provides support for
most other serial or Ethernet controlled equipment via a special
programmable device.

1.2Sequences

A
sequence is a time-based list of commands to send to the devices in
the system. A typical sequence would be something like a ‘start of
day’ which may require the following actions to occur…

After
	1 second start playing a file on a Videoserver.

After
	2 seconds turn the power on to Barco projector 1

After
	3 seconds turn the power on to Barco projector 2

It
can be seen from this list that an instruction in a sequence requires
three pieces of information, A time, a device and an operation.  The
above commands could be more succinctly written as follows.

00:00:01		Videoserver	Play
Chapter 1

00:00:02		Barco1		Power on

00:00:03		Barco
2		Power off

This
is the format used to display sequences in the scheduler. Times in a
sequence are relative to the time the sequence is started. For
example a time of 00:01:00 means 1 minute from the start of sequence.

1.3Schedule

A
schedule can be thought of as a sequence where times are based on
time of day. Sequences fall into two categories, general days and
specific dates.  General days cover the usual case where the system
runs the same everyday. Specific days let the user set a schedule
that occurs on a single day, December 25 for example.

The
following sections describe the general concepts and operation of
Easy Schedule. A device reference section explains in more detail
about the commands available for the different devices.

2The
Main Display

The
following screen shot shows the Easy Scheduler screen for a typical
installation.

The
main Easy Schedule screen is split into three main areas.

The
	left-hand window shows the Devices, Sequences and Schedules present
	in the installation. This is referred to as the Configuration
	Window.

The
	top right window is the Sequence Edit Window.

The
	bottom right window displays the message log. This log records
	everything that occurs in the Scheduler.

The
amount of space allocated to each of these sections can be changed by
clicking left over the dividing lines and dragging them around.

The
following sections describe each of these areas in more detail. These
descriptions are fairly high level. Subsequent sections will explain
how to perform specific tasks.

2.1Configuration
Window

The
configuration window contains the setup of the installation. This
includes the Devices, Sequences and Schedules. Each of these types is
covered in detail in later sections.

2.2Sequence
Edit Window

The
Sequence Edit Window is used to create and edit the contents of cues
in both sequences and schedules. This area contains the following
pieces of information

The
	currently running schedule is displayed in the “Running”
	section. This information is also replicated in the Configuration
	window if the Schedules tree has been expanded.

The
	sequence or schedule that is currently being edited is shown in the
	“Edit” section. As far as the editor is concerned, there is no
	difference between a sequence and a schedule.

The
	current time is shown in blue at the top right of the edit window.

T

he
	cues in the sequence. As described in the introduction there are
	three pieces of information required in a cue, the time, the device
	and the action to perform. This information is shown for each cue.
	The amount of information shown for each of these three fields can
	be altered by clicking left and dragging the divider lines on the
	title bar as shown in the following picture.

2.3Message
Window

The
Message window is used to record everything that occurs in the
Scheduling system. Color coding is used to distinguish the different
types of information recorded. The following broad categories are
used to distinguish the messages.

Schedule
	operational messages are shown in black. These messages record
	things such as what time the scheduler was started, what schedule
	file is loaded, when a sequence is started etc.

Executing
	Cues are shown in light gray. The Scheduler creates a log entry for
	every cue that is executed.

Warning
	messages are shown in rust. These messages show things, such as when
	a device disconnects.

Error
	messages are shown in bright Red. Error messages can be generated
	from the Scheduler, such as when a day is reached which does not
	have a schedule, or from a device driver when failures occur.

Device
	responses are shown in green. These messages are device driver
	dependant. An example message would be for a Media Matrix device
	reporting when a preset has completed.

The
message window will display the last 1000 entries received. All log
messages are also written out to disk, in a separate file for each
date.

3Devices

3.1A
brief introduction to TCP/IP

For
Easy schedule to control a device there needs to be a communications
link, that passes commands between the scheduler and the device. 
Easy schedule is designed to use TCP/IP as the communications link.
TCP/IP is a set of protocols that allow two computers (or other
devices) to pass data between them. It was made popular by the
explosive growth of the Internet for which the protocol was
originally designed. The best way to understand the concepts
surrounding TCP/IP is to think about the problem that it solves using
an example.

A
typical network will have many different computers each running many
different programs.  Imagine you are sitting at a computer and wish
to “talk” to a different computer. You need two pieces of
information, the computer you wish to talk to and the program running
on that computer.  As an example from the Internet, you may wish to
talk to Electrosonic’s main computer and retrieve a web page from
the application that handles web page requests.  In the language of
TCP/IP the following notation is used…

The
computer is referred to using an IP address.

The
application is referred to using a PORT number.

The
computer that initiates the connection (Your Computer) is called a
client.

The
computer that is connected to (Electrosonic’s server) is called a
server.

Every
computer on a network has a unique IP Address and every application
running on the same computer has a unique port number ( ranging from
1 to 65535 ). At the lowest level an IP address consists of  4
numbers between 1 and 255,  written as in the following example 
205.2.12.44.  IP addresses can also be referenced in a more human
readable form such as electrosonic.com.  mnemonic names such as these
are translated into their numeric equivalent by your computer.

Easy
scheduler acts as a client for as many servers as are appropriate for
the installation.

Using
TCP/IP as the main communication method introduces a slight problem.
How do we control RS232 based equipment such as projectors and source
machines. Easy Schedule solves this problem using a very clever piece
of equipment called a Port Server II. This box contains between 16
and 48 RS232 serial ports and a single TCP/IP Ethernet connection. 
By using this single ethernet connection Easy Schedule can talk to
all 48 serial ports as if they were local to the computer.

TCP/IP,
and networks in general, provides huge benefits for distributed show
control systems such as Easy Schedule. Locality is effectively
removed as an issue, and since networks are inherently scalable the
number of devices controlled is almost limitless. If you need another
48 serial devices, just add a new Port Server to the network.

3.2Creating a new Device

There
are two ways to create a new device for use by the scheduler…

Press
	ALT-D on the keyboard.

R

ight
	click over any device shown in the left hand tree view and select
	“New Device” from the drop down menu.

Both
these methods will produce the following dialog window.

E

very
device must have a unique name, which can be entered in the device
name field. The drop down list at the bottom of the window allows the
device type to be selected. When the OK button is clicked, the
properties for the device are displayed. These will vary depending on
the device type selected, but will usually include a tab in which the
IP address and Port number for the device will be set.

The
device reference at the end of this manual contains more details
about the properties for individual device types.

3.3Deleting
a Device

There
are two ways to delete a device from the scheduler…

Click
	left over the device in the Configuration window and press DELETE on
	the keyboard.

Right
	click over any device shown in the left hand tree view and select
	“Delete  Device” from the drop down menu

Easy
schedule will prompt you to make sure of your intention before
removing the device.

3.4Managing
Device Connections

For
Easy Schedule to be able to control a device, a TCP/IP connection
needs to be established with the device. This connection is
automatically made when the scheduler first starts running. If the
connection is lost to the device however, it will need to be reset
manually ( after the reason  for the failure is determined and fixed
).  To make the connection to all devices in the system, select
“Connect to all” from the “devices” menu. This will try to
re-establish communications to all devices in the system.

The
connection to all devices can be broken using the “Disconnect
devices” command under the “devices” menu.

The
more usual situation occurs when only one or two devices are not
connected. This would occur if the device was turned off and back on
( or rebooted in the case of a Videoserver ).

Note:
Occasionally the Port Server does not recognize that a connection has
been lost to one of it’s ports and any attempt to re-connect will
fail. If this should happen you will need to kill the connection
manually on the Port Server.

3.5Understanding
the Device Status

Devices
in Easy Schedule can be in three different states..

3.5.1.1Disconnected

A
device is disconnected if Easy Schedule does not have a valid TCP/IP
connection established with the device. This will be indicated by a
red X by the device name in the left hand tree view.

3.5.1.2Connected and working

This
state is reached when Easy schedule has a TCP/IP connection to the
device, and the device is functioning correctly.  This state is shown
by a green tick by the device name in the left hand tree view.

3.5.1.3Error

This
state indicates that Easy schedule has a TCP/IP connection to the
device and the device has indicated an error of some sort. This will
be shown by a red box with the text “ERR” , shown next to the
device name in the left hand tree view.

An
important concept to understand is the difference between the TCP/IP
connection status and the device status. A TCP/IP connection is OK if
the schedule has established a connection to the computer (or Port
Server) only. It does not imply that the device itself is correctly
functioning. Think of a valid TCP/IP connection as the same as an
RS232 cable. The fact that the cable is good does not imply that the
device connected to it is working. Easy Schedule attempts to validate
the operation of the device if it can, but there are many devices for
which this is simply not possible.

4Sequences

4.1Creating
a new sequence

It
is possible to create a blank sequence or, more usually; copy an
existing sequence. Both of these actions are achieved using a similar
process.

T

o
create a new sequence, right click over any sequence in the left hand
tree view, and select “New Sequence” from the drop down menu.

To
copy a sequence, right click over the sequence you wish to copy and
select “Copy Sequence” from the drop down menu.

B

oth
of  these actions will produce the following dialog box.

Enter
a unique name for the sequence and press OK. The new sequence will
become the current sequence in the event edit window.

4.2Deleting
a sequence

A
sequence can be deleted in one of two ways..

Click
	over the sequence name in the Configuration Window and press the
	DELETE key on the keyboard.

Click
	right over the sequence name in the Configuration Window and select
	“Delete Sequence” from the drop down menu.

In
both cases Easy Schedule will prompt to make sure you wish to
proceed.

4.3Selecting
and editing a sequence

The
events within a sequence can be edited by double clicking over the
sequence name in the Configuration Window. This will cause the events
for the sequence to be loaded into the sequence editor window. Please
see the section on editing events for details about creating and
editing cues.

The
name of the currently edited schedule or sequence is displayed above
the events list next to the word Edit:

4.4Understanding
the Sequence status icons

There
are two icons used to reflect the state of a sequence. These are as
follows..

T

he
sequence is not running

T

he
sequence is running.

4.5Running a Sequence

Sequences
can be started manually in the following way. Right click over the
sequence you wish to run and select “Run Sequence” from the drop
down menu.

4.6Stopping
a Sequence

A
running sequence can be stopped in the following way.  Right click
over the sequence you wish to stop and select “Stop Sequence”
from the drop down menu.

5Schedules

5.1Introduction

Easy
schedule manages time of day events using the concept of a day
schedule. A day schedule is a sequence of actions that should occur
at specific times during a day, with time 00:00:00 being midnight at
the start of the day, and 23:59:59 being 1 second to midnight at the
end of the day.

schedule perfor

1Introduction

Easy
Schedule is a Windows 95/NT based time of day Audio Visual control
and monitoring system designed for continuous operation without the
necessity for operator intervention.  This manual provides a brief
introduction and tutorial for using and modifying the system.

There
are three essential components to the system, devices, sequences and
schedules. These are introduced in the sections below, and more fully
explained in later sections.

1.1Devices

A
device is a piece of equipment that can be controlled by the
scheduler. A typical example would be the Electrosonic Videoserver,
or an Alcorn McBride V16 show controller.  The scheduler contains
built in protocols for a number of devices, and provides support for
most other serial or Ethernet controlled equipment via a special
programmable device.

1.2Sequences

A
sequence is a time-based list of commands to send to the devices in
the system. A typical sequence would be something like a ‘start of
day’ which may require the following actions to occur…

After
	1 second start playing a file on a Videoserver.

After
	1 second start playing a file on a Videoserver.

After
	2 seconds turn the power on to Barco projector 1

After
	2 seconds turn the power on to Barco projector 1

After
	3 seconds turn the power on to Barco projector 2

After
	3 seconds turn the power on to Barco projector 2

It
can be seen from this list that an instruction in a sequence requires
three pieces of information, A time, a device and an operation.  The
above commands could be more succinctly written as follows.

00:00:01		Videoserver	Play
Chapter 1
00:00:02		Barco1		Power on
00:00:03		Barco
2		Power off

This
is the format used to display sequences in the scheduler. Times in a
sequence are relative to the time the sequence is started. For
example a time of 00:01:00 means 1 minute from the start of sequence.

1.3Schedule

A
schedule can be thought of as a sequence where times are based on
time of day. Sequences fall into two categories, general days and
specific dates.  General days cover the usual case where the system
runs the same everyday. Specific days let the user set a schedule
that occurs on a single day, December 25 for example.

The
following sections describe the general concepts and operation of
Easy Schedule. A device reference section explains in more detail
about the commands available for the different devices.

2The
Main Display

following screen shot shows the Easy Scheduler screen for a typical
installation.

main Easy Schedule screen is split into three main areas.

The
	left-hand window shows the Devices, Sequences and Schedules present
	in the installation. This is referred to as the Configuration
	Window.

The

	everything that occurs in the Scheduler.

properties for the device are displayed. These will vary depending on
the device type selected, but will usually include a tab in which the
IP address and Port number for the device will be set.

The
device reference at the end of this manual contains more details
about the properties for individual device types.

a Device

are two ways to delete a device from the scheduler…

Click

	the keyboard.

	left over the device in the Configuration window and press DELETE on
	the keyboard.

Right

Easy

3.4Managing
Device Connections

For
Easy Schedule to be able to control a device, a TCP/IP connection
needs to be established with the device. This connection is
automatically made when the scheduler first starts running. If the
connection is lost to the device however, it will need to be reset
manually ( after the reason  for the failure is determined and fixed
).  To make the connection to all devices in the system, select
“Connect to all” from the “devices” menu. This will try to
re-establish communications to all devices in the system.

The
connection to all devices can be broken using the “Disconnect
devices” command under the “devices” menu.

The
more usual situation occurs when only one or two devices are not
connected. This would occur if the device was turned off and back on
( or rebooted in the case of a Videoserver ).

Note:
Occasionally the Port Server does not recognize that a connection has
been lost to one of it’s ports and any attempt to re-connect will
fail. If this should happen you will need to kill the connection
manually on the Port Server.

3.5Understanding
the Device Status

Devices
in Easy Schedule can be in three different states..

3.5.1.1Disconnected

A
device is disconnected if Easy Schedule does not have a valid TCP/IP
connection established with the device. This will be indicated by a
red X by the device name in the left hand tree view.

3.5.1.2Connected and working

This
state is reached when Easy schedule has a TCP/IP connection to the
device, and the device is functioning correctly.  This state is shown
by a green tick by the device name in the left hand tree view.

3.5.1.3Error

This
state indicates that Easy schedule has a TCP/IP connection to the
device and the device has indicated an error of some sort. This will
be shown by a red box with the text “ERR” , shown next to the
device name in the left hand tree view.

An
important concept to understand is the difference between the TCP/IP
connection status and the device status. A TCP/IP connection is OK if
the schedule has established a connection to the computer (or Port
Server) only. It does not imply that the device itself is correctly
functioning. Think of a valid TCP/IP connection as the same as an
RS232 cable. The fact that the cable is good does not imply that the
device connected to it is working. Easy Schedule attempts to validate
the operation of the device if it can, but there are many devices for
which this is simply not possible.

5Schedules

5.1Introduction

Easy
schedule manages time of day events using the concept of a day
schedule. A day schedule is a sequence of actions that should occur
at specific times during a day, with time 00:00:00 being midnight at
the start of the day, and 23:59:59 being 1 second to midnight at the
end of the day.

Easy
schedule performs the following actions as the time crosses midnight
at the start of a day.

looks
	for a schedule for the given date and runs it if found. This is
	called a specific day,  “June 27 1998” for example.

looks
	for a schedule for the given date and runs it if found. This is
	called a specific day,  “June 27 1998” for example.

If
	no specific date is found, the scheduler looks for a schedule with
	the same day of the week. For example if the day of the week is
	Monday, the scheduler will try to locate and run a schedule called
	“Monday”.

If
	no specific date is found, the scheduler looks for a schedule with
	the same day of the week. For example if the day of the week is
	Monday, the scheduler will try to locate and run a schedule called
	“Monday”.

If
	that fails the scheduler looks for a schedule called “Daily”. 
	If that fails no schedule is activated, and an error is reported in
	the log.

If
	that fails the scheduler looks for a schedule called “Daily”. 
	If that fails no schedule is activated, and an error is reported in
	the log.

5.5Suspending
and resuming a Schedule

In
some cases it may be necessary to suspend the currently running
schedule. When a schedule is suspended no commands are sent out to
devices when an event’s trigger time is reached, although the
schedule is still running.  This means when the schedule is resumed
any events which should have fired during the suspended time will be
ignored.

A
schedule can be suspended by selecting “Suspend” from the
“Schedule” top line menu item.  This menu item is context
sensitive so that the next time it is selected it will say “Resume”.

5.6Forcing
Easy Schedule to re-activate a Schedule

There
may be occasions where you want to force Easy Schedule to reload the
schedule that should be running on a given day.  This scenario could
occur when you wish to change what is happening today. Take the case
where the normal end of day sequence has occurred, and the schedule
has completed but there is a special event due to take place that
evening.  After editing the commands in the schedule so that they
match the time of the special event, the schedule can be restarted
using the “Activate” command under the “Scheduler” menu item.

the Schedule status Icons

completed)

T
he
schedule is active.

T
he
schedule has been suspended.

7External
Triggers

In
addition to time based events, Easy Schedule can trigger an event (
or run a sequence using the built in Sequence device ) based on a
serial message or a notification from a device. Internally serial
messages are actually dealt with as notifications from the Remote
trigger device type.

T
riggers
are entered into the system using the Device trigger dialog box. This
can be found under the Sequence topline menu item.

This
will produce the Define Device Triggers dialog box.

7.1D
efine
Triggers Dialog

A
trigger basically consists of two elements, the source of the trigger
and the action to carry out.

These
are referred as “Triggering Device” and “Triggered Device”
respectively.

Triggering
devices fall into two different types. Numeric triggers are from
devices that send the remote trigger protocol. In this protocol a
trigger is simply a number between 1 and 999. This would usually be
used with an AMX touch panel. The second type of trigger is from a
device that can send notifications when certain things happen. These
notifications are device specific and are described in more detail in
the devices reference.

8.4DataProbe
Annunciator unit

The
Dataprobe Annunciator unit provides monitoring over electrical
signals, and can provide warnings when failures occur. This device is
often linked to a video sync detector providing a warning upon loss
of video signal to an exhibit. Each Annunciator can monitor 16
signals.

8.4.1Properties

In
addition to the standard TCP/IP address, the names for the 16
channels can be entered from the following properties page.

A
name can be changed either by clicking left over a name, and then
clicking left again, or clicking left over the name and pressing the
“INSERT” key on the keyboard. This will produce a small edit
window in which the new name can be entered. Press “ENTER” to
accept the new name.

8.4.2C
ommands

The
Annunciator is a monitoring device only, and does not support any
schedule commands.

8.4.3Handling Alarms

This
section describes how alarms are reported in Easy Schedule, and how
to deal with them.

When
an Annunciator unit detects an error, it enters an alarm mode. This
unit itself shows this alarm with a flashing red light on the channel
in question and a possible audio alert if enabled. The failure is
also detected by Easy Schedule, which creates entries in the log
indicating the time of the failure and the device that failed. At
this point the failing device will show the “error” indication in
the device tree, as shown by the following picture.

W
hile
the error condition is present, double clicking over the device will
bring up a status window instead of the device properties dialog. For
an Annunciator unit this status window is as follows.

This
dialog shows a list of devices that have failed. The Acknowledge
Alarm button at the base of the window is used to tell the
Annunciator that the alarm has been noticed. At this point the alarm
light will stop flashing and the audible warning will stop. The alarm
condition will still be present in both the Annunciator and Easy
Schedule, until the source of the problem is fixed. When the problem
is rectified Easy schedule will report the time in the log and clear
the error condition on the devices tree.

The

external equipment. This allows third party equipment, such as a
touch panel, to control the operation of Easy Schedule.

The

followed by a three digit ascii number, followed by a square close
bracket. For example, [123] sends the trigger 123 to the scheduler.

parameter list.

The
Videoserver supports the following commands.

8.10.2.1PlayList

This
command is used to play the files selected by the previous PlayRange
command.

8.10.2.2PlayRange

This
command is used to select a range of files to play in a playlist. It
requires two parameters, the first file to play, and the last file to
play. These are inclusive, i.e. a parameter list of 0 1 will select
files 0 and 1.

8.10.2.3Play

This
command is used to play the currently selected file on the requested
channel.

8.10.2.4Stop

This
command is used to stop the currently playing file on the requested
channel.

8.10.2.5Seek

This
command is used to search to a given frame of the mpeg file. The
command accepts a frame number as the only parameter.

8.10.2.6Video Off

This
command is used to set the video output to black.

8.10.2.7Audio Off

This
command is used to mute the audio.

8.10.2.8Video On

This
command is used to put the mpeg video to the output.

8.10.2.9Audio On

This
command is used to put the mpeg audio to the output.

8.11Alcorn
McBride V16

This
device driver is used to control Alcorn McBride’s range of
equipment, using the Alcorn 8 bit protocol. Easy Schedule supports a
subset of this protocol, which allows a variable to be assigned to a
value in the V16. The V16 protocol also supports a reporting
mechanism that allows the V16 to send back status messages to Easy
Schedule. These messages can also be used as triggers.

8.11.1Properties

The
properties page for an Alcorn McBride V16 device is used to define
new triggers, which can be used to control sequences within Easy
Schedule. For a full description of Triggers, please refer to the
External Triggers section of this manual.

The
following screen shot shows the properties page for a typical V16
device.

A
trigger has three pieces of information

The
	Id is the trigger identifier. This value is passed in by the V16 as
	part of the message protocol. See the “Trigger Protocol” section
	for details.

The
	Id is the trigger identifier. This value is passed in by the V16 as
	part of the message protocol. See the “Trigger Protocol” section
	for details.

A
	Level indicator. This specifies how the message should be handled by
	the Scheduler logging and fault reporting mechanism. All messages
	are kept in the log files and screen log display, but the Scheduler
	also provides remote E-mail capability, which is controlled by the
	level settings. There are currently three levels, Notify, Warning
	and Error. Notify should be used for logging normal operational
	messages. Warning should be used for operations that may require
	investigation but are not major failures that disrupt the operation
	of an exhibit. Errors are usually reserved for major failures that
	require immediate attention.

A
	Level indicator. This specifies how the message should be handled by
	the Scheduler logging and fault reporting mechanism. All messages
	are kept in the log files and screen log display, but the Scheduler
	also provides remote E-mail capability, which is controlled by the
	level settings. There are currently three levels, Notify, Warning
	and Error. Notify should be used for logging normal operational
	messages. Warning should be used for operations that may require
	investigation but are not major failures that disrupt the operation
	of an exhibit. Errors are usually reserved for major failures that
	require immediate attention.

Description.
	This is used to identify the trigger in the “Edit Triggers”
	dialog. Note that this name does not appear in the log files. The
	log file text is provided as part of the message from the V16.

Description.
	This is used to identify the trigger in the “Edit Triggers”
	dialog. Note that this name does not appear in the log files. The
	log file text is provided as part of the message from the V16.

To
create a new Trigger in the table, simply fill in the three pieces of
information in the appropriate fields and click left over “Add”.
This will add the trigger to the table. If the trigger Id is already
used in the table, a warning message will appear ensuring that Id’s
cannot be accidentally replaced.

To
delete a Trigger, highlight the trigger by clicking left over it’s
Id, and then click “Delete” to remove it. A confirmation message
will appear to prevent accidental deletion.

8.11.2Trigger Protocol

This
section describes the protocol that an Alcorn McBride V16 controller
uses to communicate with Easy Schedule. These messages will not be
acknowledged by the scheduling system.

The
message structure follows the Electrosonic ES4000 protocol.

SOH

SOH

Type

Type

Header

Header

STX

STX

Data

Data

ETB

ETB

ETX

ETX

^A

^A

E

E

n1

n1

^B

^B

message

message

^W

^W

^C

^C

01

01

45

45

02

02

17

17

03

03

Where
…

n1

This
number is used to identify the message. The value is in ascii, for
example message 23 will be sent as 0x32 0x33.

Message

A
textual description of the event that occurred. The string should not
be null terminated and can be any length.

8.11.3Commands

T
he
following screen shot shows the V16 device cue edit window.

This
dialog box allows a predefined command to be selected from the drop
down list box. In the example above, the command “Audio - Low”
has been selected. Commands can be defined by left clicking over the
“Define” button, producing the following dialog.

Each
Command contains three pieces of information.

The
	Name is a mnemonic reference, which will appear in the cue.

The
	Name is a mnemonic reference, which will appear in the cue.

Variable
	refers to a variable in the Alcorn McBride V16. Note that in the V16
	programming system, called WinScript, variables start from 1 whereas
	in the Alcorn McBride 8 bit protocol, variables start at 0. For this
	reason the variables used in Easy Schedule should be 1 less than
	variables used in WinScript. For example, consider a sequence
	defined in WinScript which is triggered when variable 30 is equal to
	10. To remotely start this sequence from Easy Schedule, send a value
	of 10 to variable 29.

Variable
	refers to a variable in the Alcorn McBride V16. Note that in the V16
	programming system, called WinScript, variables start from 1 whereas
	in the Alcorn McBride 8 bit protocol, variables start at 0. For this
	reason the variables used in Easy Schedule should be 1 less than
	variables used in WinScript. For example, consider a sequence
	defined in WinScript which is triggered when variable 30 is equal to
	10. To remotely start this sequence from Easy Schedule, send a value
	of 10 to variable 29.

Value
	is the value that the variable will be set to.

Value
	is the value that the variable will be set to.

To
create a new command fill in the three fields described above and
left click on the “Add” button. This will create a new entry in
the table. Note that it is possible to create two different names for
the same variable/value combination.

A
command can be deleted by highlighting the command name in the list
and clicking left on the “Delete” button. A confirmation window
will appear to prevent accidental deletion.

A
“Delete All” button is also available for clearing out the entire
contents of the table. As for “Delete”, confirmation will be
required.

8.12Show
Clock

This
device driver is used to control the custom Large Clock developed by
Harris Productions.  This device is only in use in one installation
of Easy Schedule, and was developed as custom work for that site. The
programming information is included here for completeness.

8.12.1Properties

There
are no custom properties for this device.

8.12.2Commands

T
he
cue edit window for the Show Clock device is as follows..

To
change the command type, click left over the command window and
select an entry from the drop down “List Box”.

The
Mins. and Secs. edit windows will activate and de-activate depending
on the command selected. The following commands are available.

8.12.2.1Turn Clock On

This
command causes the clock to turn on, i.e. illuminate the digits.

8.12.2.2Turn Clock Off

This
command causes the clock to turn off, i.e. the digits will not be
illuminated.

8.12.2.3Start Countdown

This
command causes the countdown to begin from the preloaded time to 0.

8.12.2.4Stop Countdown

This
command causes the countdown to stop at the current time.

8.12.2.5Set Countdown time.

This
command is used to set the countdown starting point to a specific
time. This is the only command that uses the Mins. And Secs. fields.

8.12.2.6Countdown to next 15 Mins.

This
command is used to load the countdown starting point to the time
required to countdown to the next ¼ hour. For example if the cue is
executed at 10:04:00 the countdown time will be set to 11 minutes and
0 seconds.

8.12.2.7Countdown to next 30 Mins.

This
command is used to load the countdown starting point to the time
required to countdown to the next 1/2 hour. For example if the cue is
executed at 10:04:00 the countdown time will be set to 26 minutes and
0 seconds.

8.12.2.8Countdown to next hour.

This
command is used to load the countdown starting point to the time
required to countdown to the next  hour. For example if the cue is
executed at 10:04:00 the countdown time will be set to 56 minutes and
0 seconds.

8.12.3Typical Countdown sequence

T
he
following screen shot illustrates the typical sequence of cues used
to start  a countdown on the clock. This screen is taken from a
sequence designed to show a three minute countdown.

8.13Scheduler

8.13.1Properties

The
scheduler device does not have any properties.

8.13.2Commands

The
scheduler is a special built in device that provides control over the
general operation of Easy Schedule. The following operations are
available..

8.13.2.1Call Sequence

This
command can be used to start a sequence running. Sequences are not
interruptible, so if the sequence is already running this command
will have no effect.

8.13.2.2Stop Sequence

This
command can be used to stop a running sequence. If the sequence is
not running, the command will be ignored.

8.13.2.3Disable Device TX

This
command is used to disable the transmission of data to a remote
device. This might be used in the situation where a complex multi
sequence day schedule is in operation and you wish to lock out a
device without stopping other sequences which refer to the device.

8.13.2.4Disable Device RX

This
command is used to prevent a device from firing triggers in the
scheduler. A common use for this command would be to disable an
interactive touch panel while a large show is running.

8.13.2.5Enable Device TX

This
is the opposite operation to disable Device TX. It is used to
re-enable communications to a device.

8.13.2.6Enable Device RX

This
is the opposite operation to disable Device RX. It is used to
re-enable triggers from the device.

8.13.2.7Disable Day Schedule

This
command is used to suspend the current day schedule. This might be
used to temporarily interrupt the normal operation of the day, to run
some special event sequences.

8.13.2.8Enable Day Schedule

This
is the opposite command to Disable Day Schedule. It will re-activate
the day schedule at the current point in time. That is if the
schedule was disabled at 12pm and activated at 1pm, the next event to
execute will be at 13:00:01. All events between 12 and 1 will be
ignored.

When
a scheduler cue is created the following dialog box appears for
editing the action.

The
“command” drop down box is used to enter the action to perform,
and the “Control” drop down box is used to enter any necessary
action specific data.  In the case of the device RX/TX control this
would be a device name, and in the case of the Sequence start/stop
commands this would be a sequence name.  The available options in the
“Control” list box changes depending on the Command selected.

Click
“OK” to accept the changes or “Cancel” to abort the edit.

8.14Knox
Switcher

This
device driver provides control over the Knox range of Video/Audio
switchers. Provision is provided for separate switching of Video and
Audio. In addition, multiple crosspoints can be set from a single
cue.

8.14.1Properties

There
are no custom properties for the Knox switcher device.

8.14.2Commands

T
he
cue edit dialog for the Knox switcher is as follows..

The
Knox device supports a number of different commands, which can be
selected from the drop down “list box”.

T
he
following commands are available.

8.14.2.1Store Configuration 1

This
command causes the Knox switcher to save the current Crosspoint
settings internally to memory 1. These can be recalled later to
provide a very fast full channel switch.

Selecting
this command will cause the Crosspoint portion of the window to gray
out, because they are not needed for this cue type.

8.14.2.2Store Configuration 2

This
command causes the Knox switcher to save the current Crosspoint
settings internally to memory 2. These can be recalled later to
provide a very fast full channel switch.

Selecting
this command will cause the Crosspoint portion of the window to gray
out, because they are not needed for this cue type.

8.14.2.3Store Configuration 3

This
command causes the Knox switcher to save the current Crosspoint
settings internally to memory 3. These can be recalled later to
provide a very fast full channel switch.

Selecting
this command will cause the Crosspoint portion of the window to gray
out, because they are not needed for this cue type.

8.14.2.4Recall Configuration 1

This
command causes the Knox switcher to recall the Crosspoint settings
from memory 1.

Selecting
this command will cause the Crosspoint portion of the window to gray
out, because they are not needed for this cue type.

8.14.2.5Recall Configuration 2

This
command causes the Knox switcher to recall the Crosspoint settings
from memory 2.

Selecting
this command will cause the Crosspoint portion of the window to gray
out, because they are not needed for this cue type.

8.14.2.6Recall Configuration 3

This
command causes the Knox switcher to recall the Crosspoint settings
from memory 3.

Selecting
this command will cause the Crosspoint portion of the window to gray
out, because they are not needed for this cue type.

8.14.2.7Set Crosspoint

This
command is used to set a number of Audio and Video crosspoints on the
switcher.

To
add a Crosspoint to the cue, the following steps are required.

Select
	the required Crosspoint in the input and output edit windows.

Select
	the required Crosspoint in the input and output edit windows.

Highlight
	the radio button for either Video or Audio as required.

Highlight
	the radio button for either Video or Audio as required.

Click
	left over the “Add” Button.

Click
	left over the “Add” Button.

This
will cause the Crosspoint to be added to the Crosspoint table. The
following screen shot shows an example cue with two entries, an audio
switch connecting input 3 to output 1, and a video switch connecting
input 7 to output 4.

Entries
can be deleted from the Crosspoint table by highlighting an entry and
clicking left over the “Delete” button. A confirmation message
will appear to prevent accidental deletion.

A
“Delete All” button is also provided, allowing the entire table
to be deleted. As for “Delete”, confirmation is required to
prevent accidental deletion.

8.15Flipper
Controller

The
flipper device is a device driver written to control a specific piece
of custom software designed to display sports results on a large
mechanical “train station“ flipper style display. The device
details are included in this manual for completeness.

8.15.1Properties

There
are no custom properties for this device type.

8.15.2Commands

T
he
Cue edit dialog box for the Flipper Controller is as follows.

There
are three commands that can be sent to the flipper control software.

Disable
	Schedule. This command stops the internal scheduler from sending
	messages to the displays. This would be used in situations where the
	flipper should be stopped, during an event for example.

Disable
	Schedule. This command stops the internal scheduler from sending
	messages to the displays. This would be used in situations where the
	flipper should be stopped, during an event for example.

Enable
	Schedule. This command re-enables the internal scheduler.

Enable
	Schedule. This command re-enables the internal scheduler.

Send
	a “look” to the display. This may be used for showing special
	messages during the day.

Send
	a “look” to the display. This may be used for showing special
	messages during the day.

C
ustom
messages can be created by left clicking on the “Define” Button.
This will produce the “Flipper Controller Cue” dialog box.

The
flipper system contains 4 separate boards, each showing 50
characters. The following steps are needed to create a new look for
the four boards.

Enter
	a name for the look. This name will appear in the “Flipper
	command” dialog box.

Enter
	a name for the look. This name will appear in the “Flipper
	command” dialog box.

Ensure
	that “Board 1” is showing in the drop list at the top of the
	window.

Ensure
	that “Board 1” is showing in the drop list at the top of the
	window.

Enter
	the required text for the 6 lines on board 1. Note that this text
	will be centered when it is displayed on the flipper board.

Enter
	the required text for the 6 lines on board 1. Note that this text
	will be centered when it is displayed on the flipper board.

Select
	“Board 2” from the drop list.

Select
	“Board 2” from the drop list.

Enter
	the required text for the 6 lines on board 2.

Enter
	the required text for the 6 lines on board 2.

Repeat
	steps 4 and 5 for the remaining boards.

Repeat
	steps 4 and 5 for the remaining boards.

When
	all the text has been entered, click left on “Add” to create the
	new look.

When
	all the text has been entered, click left on “Add” to create the
	new look.

A
existing look can be edited by double clicking over it’s name in
the “Looks” list at the bottom of the window. After editing, use
“Add” to update the look. Note that a prompt message will appear
confirming you wish to update the look.

A
look can be deleted by highlighting it’s name in the “Looks”
window and left clicking over the “Delete” button. A confirmation
window will appear to prevent accidental deletion.

8.16Winwall

This
device can be used to remotely control Electrosonic’s Worksurface
control system, called Winwall. Winwall provides a simple remote
control protocol allowing different effects to be started and
stopped.

The
Winwall remote control interface supports triggering when specific
effects finish.

8.16.1Properties

T
he
Winwall properties page is used to create triggers which occur when
Winwall effects reach the end of their timeline. The Trigger
properties page is shown below.

A
Winwall trigger contains three pieces of information..

The
	id is used to identify the trigger. It should be different for each
	trigger.

The
	id is used to identify the trigger. It should be different for each
	trigger.

The
	severity of the trigger. This field is included for compatibility
	with other devices, which can report major failures. For a Winwall
	device, this should be left on Notify.

The
	severity of the trigger. This field is included for compatibility
	with other devices, which can report major failures. For a Winwall
	device, this should be left on Notify.

The
	effect name. This effect must complete for the trigger to fire.

The
	effect name. This effect must complete for the trigger to fire.

To
create a new trigger these three pieces of information should be
entered in the appropriate fields and the “Add” button selected.
If the Scheduler has a connection to the Winwall software (i.e. the
Winwall device has a green tick next to it in the Device tree)
clicking on Refresh will retrieve the list of effects in the current
Winwall show.

8.16.2Commands

T
he
Winwall device supports two commands, run an effect and stop the
currently running effect. The following screen shot shows the Winwall
cue edit dialog.

The
command is selected using the drop list (showing “Run Effect” in
the example screen above.). The Run Effect command requires an effect
name to be entered in the edit box directly below the command. The
effect name can be selected from the effects list, or entered
manually. If a connection is present to the Winwall software, a list
of effects in the current Winwall show can be retrieved by clicking
on the “Refresh” button. After a short delay the effects list
will fill with names.

Page
52
