	that is not considered “valid.” This presents some problems if
	we were to compose regular expressions in this manner. The regular
	expressions would become necessary large or at least more complex,
	therefore affecting performance. We have addressed this issue by
	coding another mechanism that deals with discarding invalid
	characters.

In the xml
	configuration file, the security or sysadmin will have to type all
	“valid” characters in the globals section. This section will
	then be applied to all items in the xml configuration that do not
	have a “valid” characters section of its own. Additionally,
	there is another section that deals with “valid” repeatable
	characters. These are characters that can appear in a string
	contiguously. By design, all alpha-numeric characters (a-z 0-9)
	space and new line characters can be repeated. In the case of space
	and new line characters that is true only if you allow space and
	multiline characters in the configuration file, for that item.
	Therefore, if you expect any other repeatable characters to appear
	in a string, you must make sure they are added to the configuration
	file.

There are a few other
	things that are adjustable through the configuration file. Such as
	character case sensitivity. If you set an item to be case
	insensitive, its PCRE data will be compiled with that option, that
	should also speed things up.

The Apache project
	XERCES XML parser library was used for PCREBinGen only, the library
	does not deal with XML at all in order to keep a small footprint.

A logging system
	similar to the one used in the neti project from HMS was used for
	output of information and other messages from the library.

4 Usage

The system was
	designed to be simple to use. Although the library is relatively
	easy to use, what takes the most time is editing the PCREBinGen.xml
	configuration file used to produce the binary file. This must be
	done very carefully and tested thoroughly to insure proper input
	validation. Fortunately, the Enterprise Security Team would produce
	prepackaged ValidateItems that would be useful in most situations
	and applications, and only those times deemed necessary should we
	produce new entries.

The xml validation
	file should

not

contain a comprehensive list of all the
	ValidateItem entries. Each application should have its own
	configuration file. This would make the library more efficient in
	its usage of memory, so that only the items that are necessary to
	validate on a per application basis would be kept in RAM.

4.1 Initial Setup and configuration

Before anyone attempts
	to use the library, the teams must agree on what's going to be
	validated and craft the necessary regular expressions carefully and
	test them thoroughly. Failure to do so may result in either a
	possible vulnerability or denial of service. After this step is
	completed, the Enterprise Security Team will prepare a
	PCREBinGen.xml file with the necessary information, then a
	libinval.pcre will be generated and sent to the development team.

Initially there should
	be 3 files that are necessary;

libinval.so: This
		is the library file and it must be found in either of these
		locations; /usr/lib or /usr/local/lib. Alternatively, the file can
		be copied anywhere and a symbolic link pointed to it can be placed
		in any of the above mentioned locations.

libinval.pcre:
		This is the configuration file and it must be found in either of
		these two locations; /etc/libinval/libinval.pcre or
		/usr/local/etc/libinval.pcre

The other file is
		libinval.h header file for C/C++ projects or InVal.pm Perl Module
		for Perl projects. Actually, in Perl there are other files that
		must be copied in order to use InVal.pm module which is a wrapper.
		Alternatively, Perl programmers can use inline C embeded in perl
		code, which may improve performance and does not necessitate the
		extra Perl files.

At this point, the
	development team must know what is contained in the binary file,
	that is, the global defaults and each of the ValidateItem entries.
	Each of this entry has a name and that is how the library identifies
	each entry which is a type of validation.

In order to increase
	the library's performance, a different way of addressing the entries
	was devised, instead of using its name. Each entry is addressed by
	an ID. This ID is not known until runtime, (well, it is really the
	numerical order of each of the entries in the XML file, but this may
	change in future versions and must not be relied upon,) therefore,
	the programmer must obtain each entry ID based on its name. The
	names are case sensitive and can be things like Email, Name, Age,
	PhoneNumber, etc... The programmer must keep a reference to this ID
	throughout the runtime of their application. These Ids must be
	obtained each time the application is started.

Although, this may
	seem like an extra step, it is a necessary one in order to avoid
	calculating this internally each time by string comparison against
	possibly all entries. This, of course, was done in order to increase
	run time performance.

PERFORMANCE TIP: Put
	items that are to be validated the most in the beginning of the xml
	file. This will save a few cpu ticks.

The input validation
	library outputs information to STDOUT and STDERR and by default will
	output at a logging level of LOG_INFO (please see next chapter for
	meaning.) Programmers may do as they please with this information,
	i.e. redirect to system log or capture output.

The library tries to
	write statistic information to a file /var/log/libinval.stats, make
	sure that the library or program has permissions to write to that
	location. Otherwise the stats will be saved in
	/var/tmp/libinval.stats

The stats file
	contains a header with enough information to understand the format.
	The content is text. The function iv_save_stats() must be called
	periodically, otherwise stats may be lost. Stats information is
	crucial in order for the teams to understand what's been going on
	during input validation and to determine if adjustments to the
	configuration file will be necessary or to act upon the information
	learned in other ways.

4.2 Usage of libinval.so in C/C++ projects

Project preparation:

Add:

#include
	"libinval.h"

to the project or file that
	would call library routines.

Program
	initialization:

Call:

idItem
	= iv_get_validate_item_id( “ValidateItem name” );

For each item. Let's
	keep in mind that at this point the programmer knows what types of
	validation exist and knows them by name. If returned value is -1 an
	error occurred or the item was not found.

Input Validation:

Call:

result
	= iv_validate_item( szStringToValidate, idItem );

Result:

1 = Validation Passed

0 = Validation Failed

-1 = An error
	condition was encountered

4.3 Usage of libinval.so in Perl projects

The syntax would be
	similar to the one above. Perl programmers can use inline C or can
	use InVal.pm which is a wrapper. When using the wrapper, there are
	other files which must be copied into either the Perl system
	library/module directory or a user local Perl directory.

The extra files are:

Inval.pm

in:
	/usr/lib/perl5/site_perl/5.8.6/i686-linux/

and:

InVal.bs autosplit.ix
	Inval.so

in:
	/usr/lib/perl5/site_perl/5.8.6/i686-linux/auto/InVal

Note: in our tests,
	when using the wrapper, there was a time increase of about 95% than
	when using C. We have not tested inline C in Perl yet but it is
	presumably faster.

5 API

From libinval.h

//return values

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_PASSED 1

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_FAILED 0

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_ERROR -1

//log options

enum

{

LOG_FATAL, 	//0

LOG_CRIT, 		//1

LOG_ERR, 		//2 *
	default

LOG_WARN, 		//3

LOG_NOTICE, 	//4

LOG_INFO, 		//5

LOG_DEBUG 	//6

};

Depending on the
	logging level selected, the library will output anything equal to or
	less than the level selected. Therefore, do not use LOG_DEBUG in
	situations other than testing, doing so will bring the library to a
	crawl, as it will have to display information on just about anything
	it's doing. Although, LOG_INFO is the default, it is recommended
	that the programmers set the log level to LOG_ERR. It was done this
	way in order to make the programmer aware of the logging level
	selected. In a “real life” situation the output may be
	superfluous since the library returns a value with the result of the
	operation and that is the only thing that should be interesting.
	This is for performance reasons as well. When the library is
	compiled for release,  LOG_DEBUG mode is disabled, this allowed us
	to gain an

… [truncated for display]

segments.content:
Report Number: A00-000-01

Application Security

Input Validation Library 1.doc

Prepared
				by:

Updated:
				08/05/2005

Lame_coyote

Version:
				0.1 RC 1

Lame_coyote009@users.sourceforge.net

Company
Name

Company
Address

Location

Table of
Contents

Table of
		Contents	2

1 Introduction	3

2 Scope, methodologies and
		limitations	3

2.1 Scope	3

2.2
		Methodologies	3

2.3
		Limitations	3

3 Application description	3

3.1 What does
		the library do?	3

3.2
		Functionality	4

3.3
		Technologies	5

4 Usage	5

4.1 Initial
		Setup and configuration	6

4.2 Usage of
		libinval.so in C/C++ projects	7

4.3 Usage of
		libinval.so in Perl projects	7

5 API	7

6 Internals	9

6.1
		PCREBinGen	10

6.2
		libinval.so	11

6.3
		PCREBinGen.xml	12

6.4
		libinval.pcre	12

7 TODO:	13

7.1 Profile and
		optimize code	13

7.2 More
		testing	13

7.3 Binary data
		encryption and checksum validation	13

8 Test results	13

9 Summary	14

1 Introduction

It seems widely
	accepted that most application vulnerabilities exist because of poor
	input validation. In fact, it is estimated that over 90 percent of
	them relate to lack or poor input validation. Therefore, an Input
	Validation Library was developed to make a programmers life easier.

This document will
	attempt to describe in detail what this libray does, how it does it,
	what problems it solves and what its limitations are.

2 Scope, methodologies and limitations

2.1 Scope

The Input Validation
	Library provides development teams and programmers with input
	validation capabilities by checking existence of input, length of
	input, value range checking for standard data types, single
	character validation for strings and PCRE (Perl Compatible Regular
	Expressions) checks.

2.2 Methodologies

The library was
	designed to work with C/C++ and Perl programs, however its use could
	be extended to any other systems, since those technologies are
	widely supported. The library was implemented in C++ using Object
	Oriented Methodologies. It's interface was made compatible with C so
	that any C application can use it by simply including its header
	file and linking with the library. Additionally, its interface was
	made available to Perl by compiling a wrapper that can be invoked by
	Perl the same way Perl would invoke any standard module.

2.3 Limitations

The library has only
	been tested as a unique running instance per computer, therefore for
	the moment it can only be used on one application per server. More
	testing will be required in order to find out if it can run multiple
	instances.

When the library
	validates input and the validation does not pass the test, this
	probably indicates some kind of tampering or hacking attempt. By
	design, the library does not know “who” is doing that, who is
	trying to hack or at least who is sending bad data. Therefore, it is
	the application's responsibility to perhaps make a note of that or
	act upon that information. Perhaps, a desirable “feature” would
	be to allow an application to pass this “extra” information to
	the library, so that the library can handle this in a “standard”
	way. At the moment the library only saves this information in a
	stats file.

3 Application description

3.1 What does this library do?

The following are the
	steps the library takes during input validation:

NOTE: “item”
	refers to a type of validation, “string” refers to the input to
	be validated.

Ensure we have a
		string and that it is not empty

Compare string
		size with global minimum and maximum requirements

Compare string
		size with item's minimum and maximum requirements

If item is a
		standard data type ensure it conforms to proper type and range

If item is a
		standard data type and has a value range ensure string is within
		proper range

If item is a
		string ensure all non alpha-numeric characters are valid

If item is a
		string ensure any repeating non alpha-numeric characters are valid

If item has a
		regular expression validate the string using the regular expression

If at any point a
	validation step does not pass, all further tests are terminated for
	that string. A more detailed explanation is provided in section 6.2.

3.2 Functionality

The Input Validation
	Libray uses a binary file as its configuration wich is compiled with
	a utility that the Enterprise Security Team developed. Let's discuss
	this utility first since it's the first part of this library.

The utility is called
	PCREBinGen and it's an executable file developed in C++. When
	PCREBinGen is run, it looks for a file named PCREBinGen.xml unless
	another name is given at the command line. A number of other
	parameters are also accepted as optional and those deal with
	settings that control how the XML file is to be processed. When
	PCREBinGen.xml is read, it is parsed and the data is processed
	internally to produce an internal document; this document contains
	global variables that were found in the xml file. If some settings
	were missing it will use its internal defaults.

The xml file must also
	contain any number of entries named ValidateItem. A ValidateItem
	entry must contain settings that control how the input validation
	library is to operate on the data that is to be validated. The
	format of the xml file is provided in Section 6 – Internals.

When the parsing is
	done PCREBinGen has an object which contains all global variables
	and all the ValidateItem entries it found. This utility will then
	process each ValidateItem and will invoke PCRE Library which it uses
	to compile any PCRE string found. The data produced by the PCRE
	compile function is appended to the ValidateItem object contained in
	the InputValidationDocument. When the utility finishes processing
	all items it creates a file called libinval.pcre. At this point
	PCREBinGen's work concludes.

The name of the
	library is libinval.so which stands for, 'lib' the standard unix
	library prefix, 'in' for input, and 'val' for validation.
	libinval.pcre is used by the Input Validation Library as its
	configuration file. The main reason that the data is compiled
	separately by PCREBinGen is to gain performance. What this means is
	that the PCRE expression is compiled only once and the data is saved
	for future use. Other systems compile the data each time a check is
	performed, this of course takes time, and although the time it takes
	is negligible when you add it all up, it can be a considerable
	length of time.

As a side effect the
	library configuration file is kept in binary format this appears to
	make the system more secure. However, is worth mentioning that in
	this version, although the data in the file is binary, not all of it
	is unreadable. Therefore, anyone with a binary file viewer can
	figure out some interesting things about the file. The library could
	be improved in the next release by including a hashing and check-sum
	function which would make the file unreadable.

At this point we have
	two files that comprise our Input Validation Library, the library
	itself and libinval.pcre. Usage information is demonstrated in the
	next chapter and configuration data is provided in the following
	chapters.

Once we have an
	application to use the library. Validation can be done for the
	following data types: (signed/unsigned) char, short int, int, long,
	long long; float, double, bool and of course zero terminated
	strings. Note: the library validates strings only, this means that
	standard data type validation is done first by converting the
	string. As a side effect from validating standard data types the
	library can also provide the converted data. However, when a string
	is validated for compliance with a standard data type the validated
	value can be returned as
the
standard data type value.

The library keeps
	counters on all validations performed, total, passed, failed, errors
	and other counters that are more specific. This information is saved
	to a file upon library exit. Developers that use the library are
	encouraged to save the counters to a file periodically, this is so
	that in the event of an application or system failure we have
	statistical information that will prove useful later.

3.3 Technologies

Gnu's g++ was used to
	compile the utility and library.

The PCRE Library from
www.pcre.org
was used to perform regular expression checks. This library is
	widely used by a large number of companies including HMS, google,
	etc... libinval.so is linked statically to PCRE library, therefore
	it is unecessary to keep a local copy on the machine where the
	library is to be used. This decreases dependencies and facilitates
	the installation and use of the library. In other words developers
	do not need to install pcre, libinval has already been linked
	statically to it. Of course a disadvantage is that when pcre
	provides upgrades they will not be used by the library. However, the
	library can be provided with dynamic linkage, in that case it will
	benefit from pcre upgrades. If the library is linked dynamically and
	the pcre compiled data binary format should change, it may be
	necessary to reproduce the libinval.pcre file to adopt the new
	format.

PCRE is quite
	ubiquitous and offers very high performance. The recommended way of
	dealing with input as you may already know is
not
by
	filtering “invalid” characters, but by discarding everything
	that is not considered “valid.” This presents some problems if
	we were to compose regular expressions in this manner. The regular
	expressions would become necessary large or at least more complex,
	therefore affecting performance. We have addressed this issue by
	coding another mechanism that deals with discarding invalid
	characters.

In the xml
	configuration file, the security or sysadmin will have to type all
	“valid” characters in the globals section. This section will
	then be applied to all items in the xml configuration that do not
	have a “valid” characters section of its own. Additionally,
	there is another section that deals with “valid” repeatable
	characters. These are characters that can appear in a string
	contiguously. By design, all alpha-numeric characters (a-z 0-9)
	space and new line characters can be repeated. In the case of space
	and new line characters that is true only if you allow space and
	multiline characters in the configuration file, for that item.
	Therefore, if you expect any other repeatable characters to appear
	in a string, you must make sure they are added to the configuration
	file.

There are a few other
	things that are adjustable through the configuration file. Such as
	character case sensitivity. If you set an item to be case
	insensitive, its PCRE data will be compiled with that option, that
	should also speed things up.

The Apache project
	XERCES XML parser library was used for PCREBinGen only, the library
	does not deal with XML at all in order to keep a small footprint.

A logging system
	similar to the one used in the neti project from HMS was used for
	output of information and other messages from the library.

4 Usage

The system was
	designed to be simple to use. Although the library is relatively
	easy to use, what takes the most time is editing the PCREBinGen.xml
	configuration file used to produce the binary file. This must be
	done very carefully and tested thoroughly to insure proper input
	validation. Fortunately, the Enterprise Security Team would produce
	prepackaged ValidateItems that would be useful in most situations
	and applications, and only those times deemed necessary should we
	produce new entries.

The xml validation
	file should
not
contain a comprehensive list of all the
	ValidateItem entries. Each application should have its own
	configuration file. This would make the library more efficient in
	its usage of memory, so that only the items that are necessary to
	validate on a per application basis would be kept in RAM.

4.1 Initial Setup and configuration

Before anyone attempts
	to use the library, the teams must agree on what's going to be
	validated and craft the necessary regular expressions carefully and
	test them thoroughly. Failure to do so may result in either a
	possible vulnerability or denial of service. After this step is
	completed, the Enterprise Security Team will prepare a
	PCREBinGen.xml file with the necessary information, then a
	libinval.pcre will be generated and sent to the development team.

Initially there should
	be 3 files that are necessary;

libinval.so: This
		is the library file and it must be found in either of these
		locations; /usr/lib or /usr/local/lib. Alternatively, the file can
		be copied anywhere and a symbolic link pointed to it can be placed
		in any of the above mentioned locations.

libinval.pcre:
		This is the configuration file and it must be found in either of
		these two locations; /etc/libinval/libinval.pcre or
		/usr/local/etc/libinval.pcre

The other file is
		libinval.h header file for C/C++ projects or InVal.pm Perl Module
		for Perl projects. Actually, in Perl there are other files that
		must be copied in order to use InVal.pm module which is a wrapper.
		Alternatively, Perl programmers can use inline C embeded in perl
		code, which may improve performance and does not necessitate the
		extra Perl files.

At this point, the
	development team must know what is contained in the binary file,
	that is, the global defaults and each of the ValidateItem entries.
	Each of this entry has a name and that is how the library identifies
	each entry which is a type of validation.

In order to increase
	the library's performance, a different way of addressing the entries
	was devised, instead of using its name. Each entry is addressed by
	an ID. This ID is not known until runtime, (well, it is really the
	numerical order of each of the entries in the XML file, but this may
	change in future versions and must not be relied upon,) therefore,
	the programmer must obtain each entry ID based on its name. The
	names are case sensitive and can be things like Email, Name, Age,
	PhoneNumber, etc... The programmer must keep a reference to this ID
	throughout the runtime of their application. These Ids must be
	obtained each time the application is started.

Although, this may
	seem like an extra step, it is a necessary one in order to avoid
	calculating this internally each time by string comparison against
	possibly all entries. This, of course, was done in order to increase
	run time performance.

PERFORMANCE TIP: Put
	items that are to be validated the most in the beginning of the xml
	file. This will save a few cpu ticks.

The input validation
	library outputs information to STDOUT and STDERR and by default will
	output at a logging level of LOG_INFO (please see next chapter for
	meaning.) Programmers may do as they please with this information,
	i.e. redirect to system log or capture output.

The library tries to
	write statistic information to a file /var/log/libinval.stats, make
	sure that the library or program has permissions to write to that
	location. Otherwise the stats will be saved in
	/var/tmp/libinval.stats

The stats file
	contains a header with enough information to understand the format.
	The content is text. The function iv_save_stats() must be called
	periodically, otherwise stats may be lost. Stats information is
	crucial in order for the teams to understand what's been going on
	during input validation and to determine if adjustments to the
	configuration file will be necessary or to act upon the information
	learned in other ways.

4.2 Usage of libinval.so in C/C++ projects

Project preparation:

Add:
#include
	"libinval.h"
to the project or file that
	would call library routines.

Program
	initialization:

Call:
idItem
	= iv_get_validate_item_id( “ValidateItem name” );

For each item. Let's
	keep in mind that at this point the programmer knows what types of
	validation exist and knows them by name. If returned value is -1 an
	error occurred or the item was not found.

Input Validation:

Call:
result
	= iv_validate_item( szStringToValidate, idItem );

Result:

1 = Validation Passed

0 = Validation Failed

-1 = An error
	condition was encountered

4.3 Usage of libinval.so in Perl projects

The syntax would be
	similar to the one above. Perl programmers can use inline C or can
	use InVal.pm which is a wrapper. When using the wrapper, there are
	other files which must be copied into either the Perl system
	library/module directory or a user local Perl directory.

The extra files are:

Inval.pm

in:
	/usr/lib/perl5/site_perl/5.8.6/i686-linux/

and:

InVal.bs autosplit.ix
	Inval.so

in:
	/usr/lib/perl5/site_perl/5.8.6/i686-linux/auto/InVal

Note: in our tests,
	when using the wrapper, there was a time increase of about 95% than
	when using C. We have not tested inline C in Perl yet but it is
	presumably faster.

5 API

From libinval.h

//return values

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_PASSED 1

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_FAILED 0

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_ERROR -1

//log options

enum

{

LOG_FATAL, 	//0

LOG_CRIT, 		//1

LOG_ERR, 		//2 *
	default

LOG_WARN, 		//3

LOG_NOTICE, 	//4

LOG_INFO, 		//5

LOG_DEBUG 	//6

};

Depending on the
	logging level selected, the library will output anything equal to or
	less than the level selected. Therefore, do not use LOG_DEBUG in
	situations other than testing, doing so will bring the library to a
	crawl, as it will have to display information on just about anything
	it's doing. Although, LOG_INFO is the default, it is recommended
	that the programmers set the log level to LOG_ERR. It was done this
	way in order to make the programmer aware of the logging level
	selected. In a “real life” situation the output may be
	superfluous since the library returns a value with the result of the
	operation and that is the only thing that should be interesting.
	This is for performance reasons as well. When the library is
	compiled for release,  LOG_DEBUG mode is disabled, this allowed us
	to gain an 8% performance increase.

//#####################################

//INPUT VALIDATION

int
	iv_get_validate_item_id (char *);

int
	iv_validate_item (char *val, int id);

int
iv_save_stats(void);

//optional

void
	iv_set_log_level( int level );

iv_set_log_level() can
	be used to adjust the library's log output level.

char *
	iv_get_last_error (void);

Error conditions can
	happen and when they do, a programmer is free to call this function
	to obtain perhaps more detailed information on why the error
	occurred.

//debugging, these
	functions only do something when compiled in debug mode

void
	iv_dump (void);

void
	iv_dump_stats (void);

These functions will
	output the global defaults and each of the ValidateItem entries and
	library validation counters respectively.

6 Internals

There are two C++
	classes which are common to PCREBinGen and libinval.so:
	InputValidatorDocument and InputValidatorValidateItem. Details on
	these and the other classes will be provided in subsection 6.5. The
	following is a list of the main classes and their respective
	explanation:

PCREBinGenHandlers

Derived from xerces
	xml parser handler class. Handles parsing and has functions that are
	called by the xerces system to notify the application and provide
	data. It uses other classes from xerces and it has the class object
	InputValidatorXMLReader, which it uses as a temporary buffer to save
	data from the xml file PCREBinGen.xml or the one selected by the
	user.

InputValidatorXMLReader

When the parser
	finishes reading the xml file this class will have the xml data in a
	InputValidatorDocument object. So, the only purpose of this class is
	to intercept the data the parser throws at us and to build the
	InputValidatorDocument object. This class also has a set of defaults
	that can and will be used should the xml file be found incomplete,
	to a point.

InputValidatorDocument

This class is the most
	important one for both the utility and the library. It contains the
	globals and all ValidateItem entries including any invalid entries
	in class objects InputValidatorValidateItem in a stl vector. This
	class has the capability so serialize itself, that is, given a file
	name (hopefully, libinval.pcre,) it will load the file and build
	itself into existence complete with globals and ValidateItem
	entries. It can also save itself, (really, no pun intended ) to
	another file. The class is versioned and it stores PCRE library
	version information in the binary file.

InputValidatorValidateItem

These objects are
	managed by InputValidatorDocument and get their content from the xml
	file ValidateItem entries. InputValidatorValidateItems actually do
	the input validation. They has data type knowledge and since all
	data is private, it has public accessor methods. These objects keep
	binary data that is produced by the PCRE library's pcre_compile2 and
	pcre_study. The actual data compilation is orchestrated by
	PCRECompiler.

PCRECompiler

This class iterates
	through all InputValidatorValidateItem found in
	InputValidatorDocument m_sPCRE class member data and calls the
	functions mentioned above. If the function call pcre_compile2
	succeeds information on the compile data is obtained such as its
	length. The data and information is saved in the
	InputValidatorValidateItem. Then this object calls pcre_study which
	is a function that will return extra data that will cause the input
	validation to be faster in some circumstances. If the study function
	returns any additional data, information about it is obtained and
	the length and extra data are saved.

BinSerializer

This
class is
	used by PCREBinGen to save and load files.

StdDataTypes

This class is used to
	handle standard data types validation and conversions. It is a
	wrapper for standard C functions.

ErrorHandler

This class is used to
	obtain system and exception error information and to process it in a
	standard and modular way. It also serves as a buffer for errors, so
	that later they can be dealt with accordingly.

6.1 PCREBinGen

This utility started
	from one of the samples included in the Xerces samples directory to
	save a lot of time. It was built from SAXPrint. SAXPrint will take a
	xml file and other parameters from the command line, will parse the
	file and output to STDOUT. It supports several character encodings
	and other options. Note that all you need to do is run the utility
	and these are just options, by default it will use PCREBinGen.xml
	from the current directory.

Usage:

"\nUsage:\n"

"   
	PCREBinGen [options] [XMLFile]\n\n"

"This program
	reads in InputValidator structures and compiles them \n"

"into binary
	format and outputs to a file for later use with the Input\n"

"Validation
	Library\n\n"

"Options:\n"

"     XMLFile
	[ " <<

g_pProgramName <<

".xml * ]\n"

"    -u=xxx  
	   Handle unrepresentable chars [fail | rep | ref*].\n"

"    -v=xxx  
	   Validation scheme [always | never | auto*].\n"

"    -n      
	   Enable namespace processing.\n"

"    -s      
	   Enable schema processing.\n"

"    -f      
	   Enable full schema constraint checking.\n"

"    -x=XXX  
	   Use a particular encoding for output (LATIN1*).\n"

"    -?      
	   Show this help.\n\n"

"  * =
	Default if not provided explicitly.\n\n"

"The parser
	has intrinsic support for the following encodings:\n"

"    UTF-8,
	USASCII, ISO8859-1, UTF-16[BL]E, UCS-4[BL]E,\n"

"   
	WINDOWS-1252, IBM1140, IBM037, IBM1047.\n"

When PCREBinGen is run
	it starts parsing the command line and sets up various paramenters
	and class objects that it will later use. If the appropriate
	parameters are input and the xml file is found it is used to
	“populate” InputValidatorDocument with Globals and ValidateItem
	entries.

A pointer to
	InputValidatorXMLReader is obtained from the PCREBinGenHandler
	object after parsing completes and a PCRECompiler object is invoked
	with the pointer as a parameter.

PCRECompiler.Compile();
is called to produce the binary data that the libinval.so will
	utilize subsequently. When the compilation concludes the class
	objects and PCRE binary data are saved to libinval.pcre or another
	file name by BinSerializer.Serialize(), and if that's the case it
	should be renamed to libinval.pcre in order to be used by
	libinval.so

6.2 libinval.so

When the library
	starts, it instanciates an object based on InputValidatorDocument,
	which at that time is emtpy. It has global variables with hard coded
	values to find libinval.pcre in the locations mentioned in the
	configuration section and the default logging level.

When the library is
	initialized, it attempts to locate its configuration file and
	outputs information regarding this operation.
InputValidatorDocument.Init();
is called which handles any
	initialization. Then I
nputValidatorDocument.LoadDocument(
	“/location/to/libinval.pcre” );
is called which will cause
	the class object to build itself based on the configuration file.

At this point the
	library is ready for use.

The programmer would
	then call:

id  =
	iv_get_validate_item_id( “ValidateItemName” );

Which is just a
	wrapper for:

int
	InputValidatorDocument::GetItemID (char *szItemName);

When the programmer is
	done obtaining Ids, Input Validation proceeds as follows:

First, please
	see next subsection to undestand the main document structure.

When the programmer
	calls: iv_validate_item(char* val, int id);

The following happens:

At
		InputValidatorDocument:

Ensure item's ID
		is within proper range

Ensure we have a
		string and it is not empty

For loop to find
		string size until global maximum size, so as not to exceed the
		global maximum string size

Compare string
		size with global minimum and maximum size

At
		InputValidatorValidateItem:

If this
		InputValidatorValidateItem item has errors exit, such as bad PCRE
		compiled data, this could only occur in exceptional circumstances,
		for example system runs out of memory or something like that.

Compare string
		size with item's minimum and maximum size

If item is a
		standard data type:

Ensure it
		conforms to proper type and range

If item has a
		value range ensure it's within proper range

If item is a
		string:

Ensure all
		characters are valid, while doing so, save repeated characters in a
		buffer, to speed things up a bit

Ensure any
		repeating characters are valid

If item allows
		spaces, well, allow spaces

If item allows
		multiline, well, allow multiline then

If item has a
		PCRE validate using the regular expression

6.3 PCREBinGen.xml

Document structure:

Tag and color
	meanings: R:red=required, O:blue=optional overrides global, The rest
	are optional for the ValidateItem entries and all are required for
	globals so they are not tagged. If an entry is not found a default
	is used from within the class.

Note: The tag names
	(within <> ) must match and are
case sensitive.

<InputValidator>
Document Root
<Globals>
Globals container
<MinStringSize>0</MinStringSize>
Minimum string size
<MaxStringSize>255</MaxStringSize>
Maximum string size
<Caseless>true</Caseless>
PCRE check will be case sensitive if
true
		<HasEncodedChars>false</HasEncodedChars>
Unused
<ValidExtraChars>@#$</ValidExtraChars>
Unused
<NumericChars>123</NumericChars>
Unused
<AlphaChars>abc</AlphaChars>
Unused
<ValidSequences/>
Unused
<AllowedRepeatingChars>abc</AllowedRepeatingChars>
</Globals>
  <ValidateItem>
List of entries for a unique type of validation
R
<Name>Name</Name>
Name identifier, used by
iv_get_validate_item_id()
R
<Type>string</Type>
Type identifier:
(string,int,long,float...)[C data types]
R
<PCRE>[A-Za-z]</PCRE>
Perl Compatible Regular Expression
O
<MinSize>2</MinSize>
O
<MaxSize>50</MaxSize>
O
<Caseless>true</Caseless>
<Multiline>false</Multiline>
<AllowSpaces>false </AllowSpaces>
<MinValue>0</MinValue>
Applicable only to
standard data types
<MaxValue>0</MaxValue>
Applicable only to
standard data types
O
<AllowedRepeatingChars/>
O
<ValidExtraChars/>
O
<ValidSequences/>
Unused
</ValidateItem>
<InputValidator>

6.4 libinval.pcre

This file contains all
	the data mentioned above. For version 1 this file contains the data
	from the class object in the way the data is handled. For example,
	strings are readable. This normally would not pose a problem, since
	it is considered that security through obscurity is not the best way
	of implementing security. However, we feel that the library can be
	improved by adding a hashing mechanism, so that the data is made
	unreadable and it would also contain checksum information in the
	file.

7 TODO:

7.1 More testing

7.2 Binary data encryption and checksum
	validation

8 Test results

Computer: DELL
	Inspiron 6000 RAM 1GB, avg CPU Load was 95%

processor       : 0

vendor_id       :
	GenuineIntel

cpu family      : 6

model           : 13

model name      :
	Intel(R) Pentium(R) M processor 1.60GHz

stepping        : 8

cpu MHz         :
	1596.370

cache size      : 2048
	KB

physical id     : 0

siblings        : 1

fdiv_bug        : no

hlt_bug         : no

f00f_bug        : no

coma_bug        : no

fpu             : yes

fpu_exception   : yes

cpuid level     : 2

wp              : yes

flags           : fpu
	vme de pse tsc msr pae mce cx8 apic sep mtrr pge mca cmov pat
	clflush dts acpi mmx fxsr sse sse2 ss tm pbe nx est tm2

bogomips        :
	3137.53

OS: Gentoo Linux
	kernel 2.6.11-hardened-r15 #1 SMP Wed Jul 6 11:13:31 UTC 2005 i686
	Intel(R) Pentium(R) M processor 1.60GHz GenuineIntel GNU/Linux

The tests were
	performed with a test utility program written in C. This program has
	a number of arrays of 10 items each. There is one array for each of
	the standard data types and one array for each of the items in our
	test xml configuration file. A total of 20, 13 standard data types
	and 7 test items. Three for loops, the first one being the control
	loop which number you specify at the command line of the test
	program. The second loop, calls the 7 test items in a random
	fashion, a total of 10 times. The third loop runs also 10 times in
	sequencial order and tests the 7 items and the 13 standard data
	types. So, each time the loop number one runs it performs 140 tests.

The data in the arrays
	is a combination of valid and invalid data, some strings being
	emtpy, some exceed the allowed size. Strings for standard data types
	have valid and invalid ranges as well as “bad” characters. Some
	strings are large. The largest string tested exceeded 65,000
	characters.

In any event. The
	release version showed that libinval.so is able to perform about
	600,000 checks per second with the test data and the test
	configuration file. In “real life” tests the results will
	probably vary, depending on what kind of data will be validated, the
	complexity of the regular expressions and the combination of allowed
	characters.

Note that not all
	tests actually make it to a PCRE check. This is because the PCRE
	library test which is the Regular Expression check is done as the
	last step. Prior to that the string is checked for availability,
	correctness of length, in some cases correctness of value and
	existence of PCRE compiled binary data in the ValidateItem.

9 Summary

This library can
	provide HMS developers with an easy to use interface to PCRE. The
	library serves as a wrapper for PCRE (
www.pcre.org
) written by Philip Hazel, which appears to have done an excellent
	job in developing it. It is used and many well known applications
	such as Exim MTA, Python, Apache, PHP, KDE, Postfix, Analog, and
	many others. Google uses it as well and they developed a C++ wrapper
	for it.

Libinval.so is more
	than a wrapper because of the extra checks it performs. Those extra
	checks allow us to write much simpler regular expressions that will
	perform presumably much faster than doing it all through PCRE.

The use of this
	library facilitates programming, since the responsibility for input
	validation is delegated to the library for the most part. It serves
	as a way to organize and consolidate knowledge which can be shared
	company wide by virtue of using standardized validations, which are
	encapsulated in the binary configuration files.

The data that the
	library produces in the stats file can also prove quite useful. This
	data can be imported into databases for statistical analysis. This
	is useful because it would allow middle and upper management to make
	decisions based on the knowledge gained from statistical analysis.
	For example, suppose in March, April and June, you get a ratio of 5%
	input validation faliures, the ratio is the comparison of failure
	with the number of HTTP requests, and, in July the ratio jumps to
	10%, most likely, “something” is going on. And, of course, a
	number from that percentage is just harmless user error. But, what
	type of error? This can be learned in more detail by looking at the
	stats information. If, for example, you have lots of validation
	failures due to “invalid extra characters,” what's going on
	here? At this point management directs systems and/or security
	departments to investigate and possibly obtain logs or other
	forensic information that could uncover the “identity” or at
	least the origin of the “bad” data. At that point it can be
	decided what can be done to remedy or mitigate this situation.

Another important
	factor to note is for example, an application is programmed to use
	the library and a vulnerability is later encountered. The
	vulnerability can be handled by editing the configuration and
	recompiling the binary file. Even in the extreme case, should the
	library have to be modified, it can be done so, without necessarily
	affecting the main application.

It is also necessary
	to say that this library will not be a cure for all, be everything
	for everybody, security all in one solution. It is the developers
	responsibility to code securely and the Enterprise Security Team to
	help doing so and to analyze code and applications for other types
	of vulnerabilities. The library was designed to validate input using
	PCRE and be as easy to use, inobstrusive and fast as possible.

We believe that it has
	met those requirements and our team highly recommeds its use and
	inclusion into all applications company wide as soon as possible.

Public Domain Information
15

blocks.title: InputValidatorValidateItemString.cpp
Input Validation Library.doc
blocks.content:
Report Number: A00-000-15

Input Validation Library.doc

Table of Contents

Table
	of Contents	1

1
	Introduction	2

2
	Scope, methodologies and limitations	2

2.1
	Scope	2

2.2
	Methodologies	2

2.3
	Limitations	2

3
	Application description	2

3.1
	What does this library do?	2

3.2
	Functionality	3

3.3
	Technologies	4

4
	Usage	5

4.1
	Initial Setup and configuration	5

4.2
	Usage of libinval.so in C/C++ projects	6

4.3
	Usage of libinval.so in Perl projects	6

5
	API	7

6
	Internals	8

6.1
	PCREBinGen	9

6.2
	libinval.so	9

6.3
	PCREBinGen.xml	10

6.4
	libinval.pcre	11

7
	TODO:	11

7.1
	More testing	11

7.2
	Binary data encryption and checksum validation	11

8
	Test results	11

9
	Summary	12

1 Introduction

It seems widely
	accepted that most application vulnerabilities exist because of poor
	input validation. In fact, it is estimated that over 90 percent of
	them relate to lack or poor input validation. The Hypermedia Systems
	Enterprise Security Team has taken proactive steps to remedy this
	situation by developing an Input Validation Library.

This document will
	attempt to describe in detail what this libray does, how it does it,
	what problems it solves and what its limitations are.

2 Scope, methodologies and limitations

2.1 Scope

The Input Validation
	Library provides development teams and programmers with input
	validation capabilities by checking existence of input, length of
	input, value range checking for standard data types, single
	character validation for strings and PCRE (Perl Compatible Regular
	Expressions) checks.

2.2 Methodologies

The library was
	designed to work with C/C++ and Perl programs, however its use could
	be extended to any other systems, since those technologies are
	widely supported. The library was implemented in C++ using Object
	Oriented Methodologies. It's interface was made compatible with C so
	that any C application can use it by simply including its header
	file and linking with the library. Additionally, its interface was
	made available to Perl by compiling a wrapper that can be invoked by
	Perl the same way Perl would invoke any standard module.

2.3 Limitations

The library has only
	been tested as a unique running instance per computer, therefore for
	the moment it can only be used on one application per server. More
	testing will be required in order to find out if it can run multiple
	instances.

When the library
	validates input and the validation does not pass the test, this
	probably indicates some kind of tampering or hacking attempt. By
	design, the library does not know “who” is doing that, who is
	trying to hack or at least who is sending bad data. Therefore, it is
	the application's responsibility to perhaps make a note of that or
	act upon that information. Perhaps, a desirable “feature” would
	be to allow an application to pass this “extra” information to
	the library, so that the library can handle this in a “standard”
	way. At the moment the library only saves this information in a
	stats file.

3 Application description

3.1 What does this library do?

The following are the
	steps the library takes during input validation:

NOTE: “item”
	refers to a type of validation, “string” refers to the input to
	be validated.

Ensure we have a
		string and that it is not empty

Compare string
		size with global minimum and maximum requirements

Compare string
		size with item's minimum and maximum requirements

If item is a
		standard data type ensure it conforms to proper type and range

If item is a
		standard data type and has a value range ensure string is within
		proper range

If item is a
		string ensure all non alpha-numeric characters are valid

If item is a
		string ensure any repeating non alpha-numeric characters are valid

If item has a
		regular expression validate the string using the regular expression

If at any point a
	validation step does not pass, all further tests are terminated for
	that string. A more detailed explanation is provided in section 6.2.

3.2 Functionality

The Input Validation
	Libray uses a binary file as its configuration wich is compiled with
	a utility that the Enterprise Security Team developed. Let's discuss
	this utility first since it's the first part of this library.

The utility is called
	PCREBinGen and it's an executable file developed in C++. When
	PCREBinGen is run, it looks for a file named PCREBinGen.xml unless
	another name is given at the command line. A number of other
	parameters are also accepted as optional and those deal with
	settings that control how the XML file is to be processed. When
	PCREBinGen.xml is read, it is parsed and the data is processed
	internally to produce an internal document; this document contains
	global variables that were found in the xml file. If some settings
	were missing it will use its internal defaults.

The xml file must also
	contain any number of entries named ValidateItem. A ValidateItem
	entry must contain settings that control how the input validation
	library is to operate on the data that is to be validated. The
	format of the xml file is provided in Section 6 – Internals.

When the parsing is
	done PCREBinGen has an object which contains all global variables
	and all the ValidateItem entries it found. This utility will then
	process each ValidateItem and will invoke PCRE Library which it uses
	to compile any PCRE string found. The data produced by the PCRE
	compile function is appended to the ValidateItem object contained in
	the InputValidationDocument. When the utility finishes processing
	all items it creates a file called libinval.pcre. At this point
	PCREBinGen's work concludes.

The name of the
	library is libinval.so which stands for, 'lib' the standard unix
	library prefix, 'in' for input, and 'val' for validation.
	libinval.pcre is used by the Input Validation Library as its
	configuration file. The main reason that the data is compiled
	separately by PCREBinGen is to gain performance. What this means is
	that the PCRE expression is compiled only once and the data is saved
	for future use. Other systems compile the data each time a check is
	performed, this of course takes time, and although the time it takes
	is negligible when you add it all up, it can be a considerable
	length of time.

As a side effect the
	library configuration file is kept in binary format this appears to
	make the system more secure. However, is worth mentioning that in
	this version, although the data in the file is binary, not all of it
	is unreadable. Therefore, anyone with a binary file viewer can
	figure out some interesting things about the file. The library could
	be improved in the next release by including a hashing and check-sum
	function which would make the file unreadable.

At this point we have
	two files that comprise our Input Validation Library, the library
	itself and libinval.pcre. Usage information is demonstrated in the
	next chapter and configuration data is provided in the following
	chapters.

Once we have an
	application to use the library. Validation can be done for the
	following data types: (signed/unsigned) char, short int, int, long,
	long long; float, double, bool and of course zero terminated
	strings. Note: the library validates strings only, this means that
	standard data type validation is done first by converting the
	string. As a side effect from validating standard data types the
	library can also provide the converted data. However, when a string
	is validated for compliance with a standard data type the validated
	value can be returned as

the

standard data type value.

The library keeps
	counters on all validations performed, total, passed, failed, errors
	and other counters that are more specific. This information is saved
	to a file upon library exit. Developers that use the library are
	encouraged to save the counters to a file periodically, this is so
	that in the event of an application or system failure we have
	statistical information that will prove useful later.

3.3 Technologies

Gnu's g++ was used to
	compile the utility and library.

The PCRE Library from

www.pcre.org

was used to perform regular expression checks. This library is
	widely used by a large number of companies including google, etc...
	libinval.so is linked statically to PCRE library, therefore it is
	unecessary to keep a local copy on the machine where the library is
	to be used. This decreases dependencies and facilitates the
	installation and use of the library. In other words developers do
	not need to install pcre, libinval has already been linked
	statically to it. Of course a disadvantage is that when pcre
	provides upgrades they will not be used by the library. However, the
	library can be provided with dynamic linkage, in that case it will
	benefit from pcre upgrades. If the library is linked dynamically and
	the pcre compiled data binary format should change, it may be
	necessary to reproduce the libinval.pcre file to adopt the new
	format.

PCRE is quite
	ubiquitous and offers very high performance. The recommended way of
	dealing with input as you may already know is

not

by filtering “invalid” characters, but by discarding everything
	that is not considered “valid.” This presents some problems if
	we were to compose regular expressions in this manner. The regular
	expressions would become necessary large or at least more complex,
	therefore affecting performance. We have addressed this issue by
	coding another mechanism that deals with discarding invalid
	characters.

In the xml
	configuration file, the security or sysadmin will have to type all
	“valid” characters in the globals section. This section will
	then be applied to all items in the xml configuration that do not
	have a “valid” characters section of its own. Additionally,
	there is another section that deals with “valid” repeatable
	characters. These are characters that can appear in a string
	contiguously. By design, all alpha-numeric characters (a-z 0-9)
	space and new line characters can be repeated. In the case of space
	and new line characters that is true only if you allow space and
	multiline characters in the configuration file, for that item.
	Therefore, if you expect any other repeatable characters to appear
	in a string, you must make sure they are added to the configuration
	file.

There are a few other
	things that are adjustable through the configuration file. Such as
	character case sensitivity. If you set an item to be case
	insensitive, its PCRE data will be compiled with that option, that
	should also speed things up.

The Apache project
	XERCES XML parser library was used for PCREBinGen only, the library
	does not deal with XML at all in order to keep a small footprint.

A logging system was
	used for output of information and other messages from the library.

4 Usage

The system was
	designed to be simple to use. Although the library is relatively
	easy to use, what takes the most time is editing the PCREBinGen.xml
	configuration file used to produce the binary file. This must be
	done very carefully and tested thoroughly to insure proper input
	validation. Fortunately, the Enterprise Security Team would produce
	prepackaged ValidateItems that would be useful in most situations
	and applications, and only those times deemed necessary should we
	produce new entries.

The xml validation
	file should

not

contain a comprehensive list of all the
	ValidateItem entries. Each application should have its own
	configuration file. This would make the library more efficient in
	its usage of memory, so that only the items that are necessary to
	validate on a per application basis would be kept in RAM.

4.1 Initial Setup and configuration

Before anyone attempts
	to use the library, the teams must agree on what's going to be
	validated and craft the necessary regular expressions carefully and
	test them thoroughly. Failure to do so may result in either a
	possible vulnerability or denial of service. After this step is
	completed, the Enterprise Security Team will prepare a
	PCREBinGen.xml file with the necessary information, then a
	libinval.pcre will be generated and sent to the development team.

Initially there should
	be 3 files that are necessary;

libinval.so: This
		is the library file and it must be found in either of these
		locations; /usr/lib or /usr/local/lib. Alternatively, the file can
		be copied anywhere and a symbolic link pointed to it can be placed
		in any of the above mentioned locations.

libinval.pcre:
		This is the configuration file and it must be found in either of
		these two locations; /etc/libinval/libinval.pcre or
		/usr/local/etc/libinval.pcre

The other file is
		libinval.h header file for C/C++ projects or InVal.pm Perl Module
		for Perl projects. Actually, in Perl there are other files that
		must be copied in order to use InVal.pm module which is a wrapper.
		Alternatively, Perl programmers can use inline C embeded in perl
		code, which may improve performance and does not necessitate the
		extra Perl files.

At this point, the
	development team must know what is contained in the binary file,
	that is, the global defaults and each of the ValidateItem entries.
	Each of this entry has a name and that is how the library identifies
	each entry which is a type of validation.

In order to increase
	the library's performance, a different way of addressing the entries
	was devised, instead of using its name. Each entry is addressed by
	an ID. This ID is not known until runtime, (well, it is really the
	numerical order of each of the entries in the XML file, but this may
	change in future versions and must not be relied upon,) therefore,
	the programmer must obtain each entry ID based on its name. The
	names are case sensitive and can be things like Email, Name, Age,
	PhoneNumber, etc... The programmer must keep a reference to this ID
	throughout the runtime of their application. These Ids must be
	obtained each time the application is started.

Although, this may
	seem like an extra step, it is a necessary one in order to avoid
	calculating this internally each time by string comparison against
	possibly all entries. This, of course, was done in order to increase
	run time performance.

PERFORMANCE TIP: Put
	items that are to be validated the most in the beginning of the xml
	file. This will save a few cpu ticks.

The input validation
	library outputs information to STDOUT and STDERR and by default will
	output at a logging level of LOG_INFO (please see next chapter for
	meaning.) Programmers may do as they please with this information,
	i.e. redirect to system log or capture output.

The library tries to
	write statistic information to a file /var/log/libinval.stats, make
	sure that the library or program has permissions to write to that
	location. Otherwise the stats will be saved in
	/var/tmp/libinval.stats

The stats file
	contains a header with enough information to understand the format.
	The content is text. The function iv_save_stats() must be called
	periodically, otherwise stats may be lost. Stats information is
	crucial in order for the teams to understand what's been going on
	during input validation and to determine if adjustments to the
	configuration file will be necessary or to act upon the information
	learned in other ways.

4.2 Usage of libinval.so in C/C++ projects

Project preparation:

Add:

#include
	"libinval.h"

to the project or file
	that would call library routines.

Program
	initialization:

Call:

idItem
	= iv_get_validate_item_id( “ValidateItem name” );

For each item. Let's
	keep in mind that at this point the programmer knows what types of
	validation exist and knows them by name. If returned value is -1 an
	error occurred or the item was not found.

Input Validation:

Call:

result
	= iv_validate_item( szStringToValidate, idItem );

Result:

1 = Validation Passed

0 = Validation Failed

-1 = An error
	condition was encountered

4.3 Usage of libinval.so in Perl projects

The syntax would be
	similar to the one above. Perl programmers can use inline C or can
	use InVal.pm which is a wrapper. When using the wrapper, there are
	other files which must be copied into either the Perl system
	library/module directory or a user local Perl directory.

The extra files are:

Inval.pm

in:
	/usr/lib/perl5/site_perl/5.8.6/i686-linux/

and:

InVal.bs autosplit.ix
	Inval.so

in:
	/usr/lib/perl5/site_perl/5.8.6/i686-linux/auto/InVal

Note: in our tests,
	when using the wrapper, there was a time increase of about 95% than
	when using C. We have not tested inline C in Perl yet but it is
	presumably faster.

5 API

From libinval.h

//return values

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_PASSED 1

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_FAILED 0

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_ERROR -1

//log options

enum

{

LOG_FATAL, 	//0

LOG_CRIT, 		//1

LOG_ERR, 		//2 *
	default

LOG_WARN, 		//3

LOG_NOTICE, 	//4

LOG_INFO, 		//5

LOG_DEBUG 	//6

};

Depending on the
	logging level selected, the library will output anything equal to or
	less than the level selected. Therefore, do not use LOG_DEBUG in
	situations other than testing, doing so will bring the library to a
	crawl, as it will have to display information on just about anything
	it's doing. Although, LOG_INFO is the default, it is recommended
	that the programmers set the log level to LOG_ERR. It was done this
	way in order to make the programmer aware of the logging level
	selected. In a “real life” situation the output may be
	superfluous since the library returns a value with the result of the
	operation and that is the only thing that should be interesting.
	This is for performance reasons as well. When the library is
	compiled for release,  LOG_DEBUG mode is disabled, this allowed us
	to gain an 8% performance increase.

//#####################################

//INPUT VALIDATION

int
	iv_get_validate_item_id (char *);

int
	iv_validate_item (char *val, int id);

int

iv_save_stats(void);

//optional

void
	iv_set_log_lev

… [truncated for display]

segments.content:
Report Number: A00-000-15

Input Validation Library.doc

Table of Contents

Table
	of Contents	1

1
	Introduction	2

2
	Scope, methodologies and limitations	2

2.1
	Scope	2

2.2
	Methodologies	2

2.3
	Limitations	2

3
	Application description	2

3.1
	What does this library do?	2

3.2
	Functionality	3

3.3
	Technologies	4

4
	Usage	5

4.1
	Initial Setup and configuration	5

4.2
	Usage of libinval.so in C/C++ projects	6

4.3
	Usage of libinval.so in Perl projects	6

5
	API	7

6
	Internals	8

6.1
	PCREBinGen	9

6.2
	libinval.so	9

6.3
	PCREBinGen.xml	10

6.4
	libinval.pcre	11

7
	TODO:	11

7.1
	More testing	11

7.2
	Binary data encryption and checksum validation	11

8
	Test results	11

9
	Summary	12

1 Introduction

It seems widely
	accepted that most application vulnerabilities exist because of poor
	input validation. In fact, it is estimated that over 90 percent of
	them relate to lack or poor input validation. The Hypermedia Systems
	Enterprise Security Team has taken proactive steps to remedy this
	situation by developing an Input Validation Library.

This document will
	attempt to describe in detail what this libray does, how it does it,
	what problems it solves and what its limitations are.

2 Scope, methodologies and limitations

2.1 Scope

The Input Validation
	Library provides development teams and programmers with input
	validation capabilities by checking existence of input, length of
	input, value range checking for standard data types, single
	character validation for strings and PCRE (Perl Compatible Regular
	Expressions) checks.

2.2 Methodologies

The library was
	designed to work with C/C++ and Perl programs, however its use could
	be extended to any other systems, since those technologies are
	widely supported. The library was implemented in C++ using Object
	Oriented Methodologies. It's interface was made compatible with C so
	that any C application can use it by simply including its header
	file and linking with the library. Additionally, its interface was
	made available to Perl by compiling a wrapper that can be invoked by
	Perl the same way Perl would invoke any standard module.

2.3 Limitations

The library has only
	been tested as a unique running instance per computer, therefore for
	the moment it can only be used on one application per server. More
	testing will be required in order to find out if it can run multiple
	instances.

When the library
	validates input and the validation does not pass the test, this
	probably indicates some kind of tampering or hacking attempt. By
	design, the library does not know “who” is doing that, who is
	trying to hack or at least who is sending bad data. Therefore, it is
	the application's responsibility to perhaps make a note of that or
	act upon that information. Perhaps, a desirable “feature” would
	be to allow an application to pass this “extra” information to
	the library, so that the library can handle this in a “standard”
	way. At the moment the library only saves this information in a
	stats file.

3 Application description

3.1 What does this library do?

The following are the
	steps the library takes during input validation:

NOTE: “item”
	refers to a type of validation, “string” refers to the input to
	be validated.

Ensure we have a
		string and that it is not empty

Compare string
		size with global minimum and maximum requirements

Compare string
		size with item's minimum and maximum requirements

If item is a
		standard data type ensure it conforms to proper type and range

If item is a
		standard data type and has a value range ensure string is within
		proper range

If item is a
		string ensure all non alpha-numeric characters are valid

If item is a
		string ensure any repeating non alpha-numeric characters are valid

If item has a
		regular expression validate the string using the regular expression

If at any point a
	validation step does not pass, all further tests are terminated for
	that string. A more detailed explanation is provided in section 6.2.

3.2 Functionality

The Input Validation
	Libray uses a binary file as its configuration wich is compiled with
	a utility that the Enterprise Security Team developed. Let's discuss
	this utility first since it's the first part of this library.

The utility is called
	PCREBinGen and it's an executable file developed in C++. When
	PCREBinGen is run, it looks for a file named PCREBinGen.xml unless
	another name is given at the command line. A number of other
	parameters are also accepted as optional and those deal with
	settings that control how the XML file is to be processed. When
	PCREBinGen.xml is read, it is parsed and the data is processed
	internally to produce an internal document; this document contains
	global variables that were found in the xml file. If some settings
	were missing it will use its internal defaults.

The xml file must also
	contain any number of entries named ValidateItem. A ValidateItem
	entry must contain settings that control how the input validation
	library is to operate on the data that is to be validated. The
	format of the xml file is provided in Section 6 – Internals.

When the parsing is
	done PCREBinGen has an object which contains all global variables
	and all the ValidateItem entries it found. This utility will then
	process each ValidateItem and will invoke PCRE Library which it uses
	to compile any PCRE string found. The data produced by the PCRE
	compile function is appended to the ValidateItem object contained in
	the InputValidationDocument. When the utility finishes processing
	all items it creates a file called libinval.pcre. At this point
	PCREBinGen's work concludes.

The name of the
	library is libinval.so which stands for, 'lib' the standard unix
	library prefix, 'in' for input, and 'val' for validation.
	libinval.pcre is used by the Input Validation Library as its
	configuration file. The main reason that the data is compiled
	separately by PCREBinGen is to gain performance. What this means is
	that the PCRE expression is compiled only once and the data is saved
	for future use. Other systems compile the data each time a check is
	performed, this of course takes time, and although the time it takes
	is negligible when you add it all up, it can be a considerable
	length of time.

As a side effect the
	library configuration file is kept in binary format this appears to
	make the system more secure. However, is worth mentioning that in
	this version, although the data in the file is binary, not all of it
	is unreadable. Therefore, anyone with a binary file viewer can
	figure out some interesting things about the file. The library could
	be improved in the next release by including a hashing and check-sum
	function which would make the file unreadable.

At this point we have
	two files that comprise our Input Validation Library, the library
	itself and libinval.pcre. Usage information is demonstrated in the
	next chapter and configuration data is provided in the following
	chapters.

Once we have an
	application to use the library. Validation can be done for the
	following data types: (signed/unsigned) char, short int, int, long,
	long long; float, double, bool and of course zero terminated
	strings. Note: the library validates strings only, this means that
	standard data type validation is done first by converting the
	string. As a side effect from validating standard data types the
	library can also provide the converted data. However, when a string
	is validated for compliance with a standard data type the validated
	value can be returned as
the
standard data type value.

The library keeps
	counters on all validations performed, total, passed, failed, errors
	and other counters that are more specific. This information is saved
	to a file upon library exit. Developers that use the library are
	encouraged to save the counters to a file periodically, this is so
	that in the event of an application or system failure we have
	statistical information that will prove useful later.

3.3 Technologies

Gnu's g++ was used to
	compile the utility and library.

The PCRE Library from
www.pcre.org
was used to perform regular expression checks. This library is
	widely used by a large number of companies including google, etc...
	libinval.so is linked statically to PCRE library, therefore it is
	unecessary to keep a local copy on the machine where the library is
	to be used. This decreases dependencies and facilitates the
	installation and use of the library. In other words developers do
	not need to install pcre, libinval has already been linked
	statically to it. Of course a disadvantage is that when pcre
	provides upgrades they will not be used by the library. However, the
	library can be provided with dynamic linkage, in that case it will
	benefit from pcre upgrades. If the library is linked dynamically and
	the pcre compiled data binary format should change, it may be
	necessary to reproduce the libinval.pcre file to adopt the new
	format.

PCRE is quite
	ubiquitous and offers very high performance. The recommended way of
	dealing with input as you may already know is
not
by filtering “invalid” characters, but by discarding everything
	that is not considered “valid.” This presents some problems if
	we were to compose regular expressions in this manner. The regular
	expressions would become necessary large or at least more complex,
	therefore affecting performance. We have addressed this issue by
	coding another mechanism that deals with discarding invalid
	characters.

In the xml
	configuration file, the security or sysadmin will have to type all
	“valid” characters in the globals section. This section will
	then be applied to all items in the xml configuration that do not
	have a “valid” characters section of its own. Additionally,
	there is another section that deals with “valid” repeatable
	characters. These are characters that can appear in a string
	contiguously. By design, all alpha-numeric characters (a-z 0-9)
	space and new line characters can be repeated. In the case of space
	and new line characters that is true only if you allow space and
	multiline characters in the configuration file, for that item.
	Therefore, if you expect any other repeatable characters to appear
	in a string, you must make sure they are added to the configuration
	file.

There are a few other
	things that are adjustable through the configuration file. Such as
	character case sensitivity. If you set an item to be case
	insensitive, its PCRE data will be compiled with that option, that
	should also speed things up.

The Apache project
	XERCES XML parser library was used for PCREBinGen only, the library
	does not deal with XML at all in order to keep a small footprint.

A logging system was
	used for output of information and other messages from the library.

4 Usage

The system was
	designed to be simple to use. Although the library is relatively
	easy to use, what takes the most time is editing the PCREBinGen.xml
	configuration file used to produce the binary file. This must be
	done very carefully and tested thoroughly to insure proper input
	validation. Fortunately, the Enterprise Security Team would produce
	prepackaged ValidateItems that would be useful in most situations
	and applications, and only those times deemed necessary should we
	produce new entries.

The xml validation
	file should
not
contain a comprehensive list of all the
	ValidateItem entries. Each application should have its own
	configuration file. This would make the library more efficient in
	its usage of memory, so that only the items that are necessary to
	validate on a per application basis would be kept in RAM.

4.1 Initial Setup and configuration

Before anyone attempts
	to use the library, the teams must agree on what's going to be
	validated and craft the necessary regular expressions carefully and
	test them thoroughly. Failure to do so may result in either a
	possible vulnerability or denial of service. After this step is
	completed, the Enterprise Security Team will prepare a
	PCREBinGen.xml file with the necessary information, then a
	libinval.pcre will be generated and sent to the development team.

Initially there should
	be 3 files that are necessary;

libinval.so: This
		is the library file and it must be found in either of these
		locations; /usr/lib or /usr/local/lib. Alternatively, the file can
		be copied anywhere and a symbolic link pointed to it can be placed
		in any of the above mentioned locations.

libinval.pcre:
		This is the configuration file and it must be found in either of
		these two locations; /etc/libinval/libinval.pcre or
		/usr/local/etc/libinval.pcre

The other file is
		libinval.h header file for C/C++ projects or InVal.pm Perl Module
		for Perl projects. Actually, in Perl there are other files that
		must be copied in order to use InVal.pm module which is a wrapper.
		Alternatively, Perl programmers can use inline C embeded in perl
		code, which may improve performance and does not necessitate the
		extra Perl files.

At this point, the
	development team must know what is contained in the binary file,
	that is, the global defaults and each of the ValidateItem entries.
	Each of this entry has a name and that is how the library identifies
	each entry which is a type of validation.

In order to increase
	the library's performance, a different way of addressing the entries
	was devised, instead of using its name. Each entry is addressed by
	an ID. This ID is not known until runtime, (well, it is really the
	numerical order of each of the entries in the XML file, but this may
	change in future versions and must not be relied upon,) therefore,
	the programmer must obtain each entry ID based on its name. The
	names are case sensitive and can be things like Email, Name, Age,
	PhoneNumber, etc... The programmer must keep a reference to this ID
	throughout the runtime of their application. These Ids must be
	obtained each time the application is started.

Although, this may
	seem like an extra step, it is a necessary one in order to avoid
	calculating this internally each time by string comparison against
	possibly all entries. This, of course, was done in order to increase
	run time performance.

PERFORMANCE TIP: Put
	items that are to be validated the most in the beginning of the xml
	file. This will save a few cpu ticks.

The input validation
	library outputs information to STDOUT and STDERR and by default will
	output at a logging level of LOG_INFO (please see next chapter for
	meaning.) Programmers may do as they please with this information,
	i.e. redirect to system log or capture output.

The library tries to
	write statistic information to a file /var/log/libinval.stats, make
	sure that the library or program has permissions to write to that
	location. Otherwise the stats will be saved in
	/var/tmp/libinval.stats

The stats file
	contains a header with enough information to understand the format.
	The content is text. The function iv_save_stats() must be called
	periodically, otherwise stats may be lost. Stats information is
	crucial in order for the teams to understand what's been going on
	during input validation and to determine if adjustments to the
	configuration file will be necessary or to act upon the information
	learned in other ways.

4.2 Usage of libinval.so in C/C++ projects

Project preparation:

Add:
#include
	"libinval.h"
to the project or file
	that would call library routines.

Program
	initialization:

Call:
idItem
	= iv_get_validate_item_id( “ValidateItem name” );

For each item. Let's
	keep in mind that at this point the programmer knows what types of
	validation exist and knows them by name. If returned value is -1 an
	error occurred or the item was not found.

Input Validation:

Call:
result
	= iv_validate_item( szStringToValidate, idItem );

Result:

1 = Validation Passed

0 = Validation Failed

-1 = An error
	condition was encountered

4.3 Usage of libinval.so in Perl projects

The syntax would be
	similar to the one above. Perl programmers can use inline C or can
	use InVal.pm which is a wrapper. When using the wrapper, there are
	other files which must be copied into either the Perl system
	library/module directory or a user local Perl directory.

The extra files are:

Inval.pm

in:
	/usr/lib/perl5/site_perl/5.8.6/i686-linux/

and:

InVal.bs autosplit.ix
	Inval.so

in:
	/usr/lib/perl5/site_perl/5.8.6/i686-linux/auto/InVal

Note: in our tests,
	when using the wrapper, there was a time increase of about 95% than
	when using C. We have not tested inline C in Perl yet but it is
	presumably faster.

5 API

From libinval.h

//return values

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_PASSED 1

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_FAILED 0

#define
	IV_VALIDATION_ERROR -1

//log options

enum

{

LOG_FATAL, 	//0

LOG_CRIT, 		//1

LOG_ERR, 		//2 *
	default

LOG_WARN, 		//3

LOG_NOTICE, 	//4

LOG_INFO, 		//5

LOG_DEBUG 	//6

};

Depending on the
	logging level selected, the library will output anything equal to or
	less than the level selected. Therefore, do not use LOG_DEBUG in
	situations other than testing, doing so will bring the library to a
	crawl, as it will have to display information on just about anything
	it's doing. Although, LOG_INFO is the default, it is recommended
	that the programmers set the log level to LOG_ERR. It was done this
	way in order to make the programmer aware of the logging level
	selected. In a “real life” situation the output may be
	superfluous since the library returns a value with the result of the
	operation and that is the only thing that should be interesting.
	This is for performance reasons as well. When the library is
	compiled for release,  LOG_DEBUG mode is disabled, this allowed us
	to gain an 8% performance increase.

//#####################################

//INPUT VALIDATION

int
	iv_get_validate_item_id (char *);

int
	iv_validate_item (char *val, int id);

int
iv_save_stats(void);

//optional

void
	iv_set_log_level( int level );

iv_set_log_level() can
	be used to adjust the library's log output level.

char *
	iv_get_last_error (void);

Error conditions can
	happen and when they do, a programmer is free to call this function
	to obtain perhaps more detailed information on why the error
	occurred.

//debugging, these
	functions only do something when compiled in debug mode

void
	iv_dump (void);

void
	iv_dump_stats (void);

These functions will
	output the global defaults and each of the ValidateItem entries and
	library validation counters respectively.

6 Internals

There are two C++
	classes which are common to PCREBinGen and libinval.so:
	InputValidatorDocument and InputValidatorValidateItem. Details on
	these and the other classes will be provided in subsection 6.5. The
	following is a list of the main classes and their respective
	explanation:

PCREBinGenHandlers

Derived from xerces
	xml parser handler class. Handles parsing and has functions that are
	called by the xerces system to notify the application and provide
