1Introduction

1.1Purpose
of Document

This document is used
to prototype an application. It will list modules, database
structure, functions, processes, application features, GUI screens,
technologies involved, work and data flow diagrams, user
interactions, automatic processes, communications, logging and
notification capabilities, application limitations, time estimates
and any foreseeable problems or recommendations.

It will also list
configuration steps, application setup and routine maintenance if
applicable.

1.2Intended
Audience

Project Managers

Programmers

System Testers

Database Administrators

Telvista Management and/or
	Administration Personnel

Partner Company Personnel involved
	with this application

1.3Scope

This
document provides detailed information on the application, modules,
interfaces and database structure. However, because there may be
other people involved in the design process anything may be subject
to change. Changes should be coordinated and reflected in this
document.

We
should make any attempt to list any information that is relevant.
Nevertheless there may be some omissions. We should make every
attempt to minimize omissions but if something has to be omitted we
should note it. This is so that we prevent time costly assumptions.

It
is assumed that the intended audience is able to understand most of
the technical terminology. Therefore, some details are omitted. While
some terms are typically only understood by programmers or database
administrators the definitions of those terms or explanations of
those terms may be omitted so that the size of this document can
remain manageable.

Only
in those cases dimmed necessary will those terms or procedures be
explained in more detail, non technical terms or at all. However, any
members of the audience’s contributions are necessary and welcome
to the success of the application. Therefore, in those cases, they
should ask the questions and we should try to clarify them.

1.4Terminology

The
word module is used loosely to mean either an application module
which can correspond directly with a GUI screen or operation mode.
Or, it could mean a function module which could also be a sub-module.
This is to be determined based on the context.

1.5Document
Structure

Standard.

1.6Related
Documents

PowerPoint
	Presentation

with initial work flow diagram

Access
	Database

with database structure prototype

2Overview

2.1General

This
call center application will be used to setup, track, and follow-up
appointments. The appointments can be re-scheduled. The appointments
are to be confirmed two days prior to the date. The record is updated
once the appointment is met. In order to do this, the system calls
the client and the agent will request information from the client.

The
agents through a predictive dialing system receive inbound and
outbound calls.

There are 2 inbound
modules:

2.1.1Inbound
- Appointment Scheduling Agent Module:

Adding a client
		or editing client information

Client screening
		and service approval

Appointment
		scheduling or re-scheduling

Prospect referral

2.1.2Inbound
- Appointment Re-Scheduling Executive Agent Module:

Appointment
	re-scheduling

There are 3 outbound
modules:

2.1.3Outbound
- Client Solicitation Agent Modules:

This
module has 3 modes of operation which determine in which database the
data will be:

2.1.3.1Frequent Flyer

2.1.3.2Client Reference

2.1.3.3Scheduled Calls

The
modes of operation also affect how the agent will relate to the
client as far as what will be said and how it will be said, but not
how the work flow is handled. The work flow is the same, only the
underlying database will be different. Actually, the database will be
the same, but will contain a field to differentiate the mode of
operation. It will only appear that way logically, structurally it
will reside in the same table. This module will be used for:

Editing client
		(prospect) information

Client screening
		and service approval

Appointment
		scheduling

Prospect referral

2.1.4Outbound
- Appointment Confirmation Agent Module:

Verifying
	appointment

Re-scheduling
	appointments

Screening out
	clients who re-schedule more than x times. Canceling

2.1.5Outbound
- Visit Confirmation Agent Module:

Verifying visit

Polling client
	satisfaction and other information

There are 2 web
update modules:

2.1.6Web
Update - Agent Assignment Executive Agent Module:

Searching
		database based on selected criteria

Assigning agent
		to visit client

2.1.7Web
Update - Visit Outcome Agent Module:

Searching database
	based on selected criteria

Update database:
	Appointment Status, Visit Status, Contract Information, Client
	Disqualification Information if applicable

There may also be a
Reporting Module so that supervisors or administrative personnel can
monitor activity, progress, numbers, etc…

2.2

Application
Context

There are 3 possible
contexts:

C1 - Agent.
	Carries out various operations as mentioned above

C2 - Executive
	Agent. Can assign agents. May view certain reports

C3 -
	Administrative. Can view reports and change application parameters
	and data catalogs. To minimize development time the parameters and
	data catalogs can be changed from MS Access, by linking the actual
	tables wherever they may reside. Since they aren’t likely to
	change that often. Or, we could design a web module for this
	purpose.

2.3User
Interface

The
GUI will be a series of screens that correspond to the main modules.
In other words, each application module will have its own screen.
However, because the modules share similar tasks, some of these
screens will be shared by the modules. If fact, some of the tasks are
identical across the modules.

The
application and the modules should attempt to make the work as easy,
as fast and as pleasant as possible by automating and anticipating
tasks.

User Modes:

M1- Inbound -
	Appointment Scheduling

M2 - Inbound -
	Appointment Re-Scheduling

M3 - Outbound -
	Client Solicitation - Frequent Flyer

M4 - Outbound -
	Client Solicitation - Client Reference

M5 - Outbound -
	Client Solicitation - Scheduled Calls

M6 - Outbound -
	Appointment Confirmation

M7 - Outbound -
	Visit Confirmation

M8 - Web Update -
	Agent Assignment

M9 - Web Update -
	Visit Outcome

GUI Screens:

S1 - Login Screen

Every employee
		must login

S2 - Module
	Selection Screen

Application
		presents a module selection screen based on employee’s
		credentials

Every employee
		must select a screen

S3 - Call
	Disposition Update Screen

All calls must be
		logged and updated with a call disposition code

After update,
		this screen brings up another screen based on the user mode of
		operation

S4 - Client Search
	Screen - Used only by inbound module

In Appointment
		Scheduling Mode, this screen brings up Client Information Screen to
		edit or add client. Then, it can either schedule or re-schedule an
		appointment, so it brings up Appointment Setup Screen

In Appointment
		Re-Scheduling Mode, this screen brings up Appointment Setup Screen

S5 - Client
	Information Screen

Used to edit or
		add client information

Agent should ask
		client or lead for best times to call at the various phone numbers

Used to add
		client referral information. In this mode of operation some fields
		are hidden, such as credit card or earnings information

When finished
		updating this screen brings up another based on user mode

S6 -Appointment
	Setup Screen

Used to schedule
		or re-schedule appointments

It has date and
		time selection controls and it reflects if a day is unavailable for
		visits based on limitations setup by application administrator
		based on the city

S7 - Appointment
	Confirmation Screen

Used to confirm,
		re-schedule, or cancel an appointment based on a limit on number of
		re-scheduling transactions

S8 - Agent
	Assignment Record Search Screen

Search
		appointments based on diverse criteria so that supervisor or
		executive can assign an agent

S9 - Agent
	Assignment Screen

View search
		results and assign an agent to visit a client

S10 - Visit Status
	Record Search Screen

Search database
		for completed visits based on diverse criteria so that agent can
		update visit status and other information

S11 - Visit Status
	Update Screen

View search
		results and update visit information and enter contract number if
		sale was realized

S12 - Visit
	Confirmation Screen

Ask client
		questions about the visit

Processes:

P1 – Responsible
	Party Determination

P2 – Redial
	Scheduling ???

P3 – Data
	Validation ???

P4 –
	Disinterested Client Classification

P5 –
	Disqualified Client Classification

P6 – Failed
	Visit Classification

P7 – Canceling
	Appointments

The
following is a list of all operations and their relationships:

Operation

Used In

GUI Type

2.4Technologies
Used

Microsoft ASP.NET

MS SQL Server

Predictive Dialing System

Multi Tier Architecture

CSS – Cascading Style Sheets

User Friendly Graphical User Interface

3Process
Breakdown

3.1Logical
Data Flow

See

PowerPoint
Presentation

related document above for preliminary
information.

For all Modes:  S1 -
Login



S2 - Mode Selection

M1 – Inbound -
	Appointment Scheduling

Call begins

S3 – Call
		classification: If invalid, update record, end

S4 – Client
		search: Obtain telephone number from caller id string, or ask
		client for acct. no. and search database. If not found add a new
		client

If client has a
		pending appointment, and if he wants to re-schedule go to S6 –
		Appointment Setup. If not re-scheduling add notes on call

If no pending
		appointment go to S5 – Client Information

If client
		disqualified, P5, end call

If client
		uninterested, P4, end call

M2 – Inbound -
	Appointment Re-Scheduling

M3, M4, M5 –
	Outbound - Client Solicitation

M6 – Outbound -
	Appointment Confirmation

M7 – Outbound -
	Visit Confirmation

M8 – Web Update
	- Agent Assignment

M9 – Web Update
	- Visit Outcome

3.2Data
Definitions

See

Access
Database

related document above for complete details.

3.3Components

3.4Dependencies

Breakdown

4.2Module
Hierarchy

		scheduling or re-scheduling

Prospect referral

2.1.2Inbound
- Appointment Re-Scheduling Executive Agent Module:

Appointment
	re-scheduling

There are 3 outbound
modules:

2.1.3Outbound
- Client Solicitation Agent Modules:

This
module has 3 modes of operation which determine in which database the
data will be:

2.1.3.1Frequent Flyer

2.1.3.2Client Reference

2.1.3.3Scheduled Calls

The
modes of operation also affect how the agent will relate to the
client as far as what will be said and how it will be said, but not
how the work flow is handled. The work flow is the same, only the
underlying database will be different. Actually, the database will be
the same, but will contain a field to differentiate the mode of
operation. It will only appear that way logically, structurally it
will reside in the same table. This module will be used for:

Editing client
		(prospect) information

Client screening
		and service approval

Appointment
		scheduling

Prospect referral

2.1.4Outbound
- Appointment Confirmation Agent Module:

Verifying
	appointment

Re-scheduling
	appointments

Screening out
	clients who re-schedule more than x times. Canceling

2.1.5Outbound
- Visit Confirmation Agent Module:

Verifying visit

Polling client
	satisfaction and other information

There are 2 web
update modules:

2.1.6Web
Update - Agent Assignment Executive Agent Module:

Searching
		database based on selected criteria

Assigning agent
		to visit client

2.1.7Web
Update - Visit Outcome Agent Module:

Searching database
	based on selected criteria

Update database:
	Appointment Status, Visit Status, Contract Information, Client
	Disqualification Information if applicable

There may also be a
Reporting Module so that supervisors or administrative personnel can
monitor activity, progress, numbers, etc…

2.2
Application
Context

There are 3 possible
contexts:

C1 - Agent.
	Carries out various operations as mentioned above

C2 - Executive
	Agent. Can assign agents. May view certain reports

C3 -
	Administrative. Can view reports and change application parameters
	and data catalogs. To minimize development time the parameters and
	data catalogs can be changed from MS Access, by linking the actual
	tables wherever they may reside. Since they aren’t likely to
	change that often. Or, we could design a web module for this
	purpose.

2.3User
Interface

The
GUI will be a series of screens that correspond to the main modules.
In other words, each application module will have its own screen.
However, because the modules share similar tasks, some of these
screens will be shared by the modules. If fact, some of the tasks are
identical across the modules.

The
application and the modules should attempt to make the work as easy,
as fast and as pleasant as possible by automating and anticipating
tasks.

User Modes:

M1- Inbound -
	Appointment Scheduling

M2 - Inbound -
	Appointment Re-Scheduling

M3 - Outbound -
	Client Solicitation - Frequent Flyer

M4 - Outbound -
	Client Solicitation - Client Reference

M5 - Outbound -
	Client Solicitation - Scheduled Calls

M6 - Outbound -
	Appointment Confirmation

M7 - Outbound -
	Visit Confirmation

M8 - Web Update -
	Agent Assignment

M9 - Web Update -
	Visit Outcome

GUI Screens:

S1 - Login Screen
Every employee
		must login
S2 - Module
	Selection Screen
Application
		presents a module selection screen based on employee’s
		credentials
Every employee
		must select a screen
S3 - Call
	Disposition Update Screen
All calls must be
		logged and updated with a call disposition code
After update,
		this screen brings up another screen based on the user mode of
		operation
S4 - Client Search
	Screen - Used only by inbound module
In Appointment
		Scheduling Mode, this screen brings up Client Information Screen to
		edit or add client. Then, it can either schedule or re-schedule an
		appointment, so it brings up Appointment Setup Screen
In Appointment
		Re-Scheduling Mode, this screen brings up Appointment Setup Screen
S5 - Client
	Information Screen
Used to edit or
		add client information
Agent should ask
		client or lead for best times to call at the various phone numbers
Used to add
		client referral information. In this mode of operation some fields
		are hidden, such as credit card or earnings information
When finished
		updating this screen brings up another based on user mode
S6 -Appointment
	Setup Screen
Used to schedule
		or re-schedule appointments
It has date and
		time selection controls and it reflects if a day is unavailable for
		visits based on limitations setup by application administrator
		based on the city
S7 - Appointment
	Confirmation Screen
Used to confirm,
		re-schedule, or cancel an appointment based on a limit on number of
		re-scheduling transactions
S8 - Agent

Search
		appointments based on diverse criteria so that supervisor or

S9 - Agent

View search
		results and assign an agent to visit a client
S10 - Visit Status

Search database

S11 - Visit Status
	Update Screen
View search
		results and update visit information and enter contract number if
		sale was realized
S12 - Visit
	Confirmation Screen
Ask client
		questions about the visit

blocks.title: README.txt
blocks.content:
LIST OF APPS:

    - Gemini: Automation Framework to instrument virtual and non-virtual servers, workstations and applicances, such as ESX, HBR
    - SSH Connector: Used by Gemini to securely connect and send commands to devices above
    - Log Framework: Used by Gemini to log to terminal or html documents, see html file for sample output.

LIST OF TECHNOLOGIES:

    - Python
    - Perl
    - Virtualization
    - Networking
    - Automation
    - QA
    - Script Instrumentation
    - Framework Development
    - Test Development
    - SSH

WORK PERFORMED:

    - Architected, designed and implemented a new testing framework for the QA department.
    - Replaced old framework mostly perl based with a lot of python. Original framework was over 80kloc, new framework is 8kloc, 8 times faster. Used object orientation, functional and meta programming as paradigms.

blocks.title: readme.txt
blocks.content:
Replacement architecture for ~60k loc

System to allow writing of tests for most functionality of vmware esx server

Features yaml configuration files, colorful logs, encapsulation of functionality, 

There are two folders with very similar structure, the reason for that is so that
control can be exercized over what the user(developer) has access to, and otherwise
make things that aren't supposed to be accessed obviously difficult, this ironically
with the intent for better usability, so the user sees less code, although, in reality
everything should happen in the test file and the rest is framework background functionality.

Please ask me for any other details.

blocks.title: base_test.py
blocks.content:
import unittest
import sys
import ssl
from lib.base_test_helper.utility import *
from lib.base_test_helper.directories import *
from lib.utility import log
from lib.utility import configuration
from lib.utility import add_unittest_asserts
from lib.utility.configuration import consolidate_files
from lib.utility.current_class import CurrentClass
from lib.framework.factory import Factory
from lib.framework.controller import Controller
from lib.lwrdo.factory import Factory as VMTFactory
from lib.service.wait import wait
from lib.service.events import Events
from lib.utility.log import clear_tabs
from lib.base_test_helper.unhandled_exception_handler import all_exceptions_hander

@log.class_logger
class BaseTest(unittest.TestCase, configuration.Configuration):
    setattr(CurrentClass(), 'name', 'None')
    delete = None
    deleteCalled = False
    isPythonVersion2_6 = False
    if sys.version_info[:2] == (2, 6):
        isPythonVersion2_6 = True

    @log.test_class_function
    def run(self, result=None):
        self.currentResult = clearCounters(result)
        unittest.TestCase.run(self, result)

    @log.test_class_function
    def __init__(self, testFile, *args, **kwargs):
        sys.excepthook = all_exceptions_hander
        log.testCase = self
        BaseTest.delete = self._delete
        self._fixPython3SslIssue()
        self.wait = wait

        self.currentResult = None
        self.startDate = datetime.datetime.today()
        self.startTime = int(time.time())

        unittest.TestCase.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)
        add_unittest_asserts.global_test_case = self
        add_unittest_asserts.isPythonVersion2_6 = BaseTest.isPythonVersion2_6

        self._calcTestFileInformation(testFile)
        yamlFile = consolidate_files(self.path)
        configuration.Configuration.__init__(self, yamlFile)
        self._calcTestDirectory()

        self.logger = setupLogger(self, self.config, self.testDirectory)

        printHeader(self.name, self.startDate, self.logger)

        self.preConfigure()
        self.writeConfiguration()

        self.logger.note('Build phase')
        self.actors = Factory.addActorsNames()
        self.mediators = Factory.addMediatorsNames()
        clear_tabs()
        Factory.build(self)
        Events().actors = self.actors
        Controller.setActors(self.actors)

        self.logger.note('Post build phase')
        clear_tabs()
        self.postConfigure()

        self.logger.note('Initialization phase')
        clear_tabs()
        VMTFactory.build(self.actors)

        self.logger.note('Configuration phase')
        # assert 1==0
        clear_tabs()
        self._configure()
        # assert 1==0
        self.logger.note('Post-Initialization phase')
        clear_tabs()
        self.postInitialize()

        self.logger.note('Verification phase')
        clear_tabs()
        Controller.check()
        self.logger.note('Object creation phase')
        clear_tabs()
        Controller.create()

        self.logger.note('Starting test run')
        printTestHeader(self.name)

    def preConfigure(self):
        pass

    def postConfigure(self):
        pass

    def postInitialize(self):
        pass

    def cleanup(self):
        pass

    @classmethod
    @log.test_class_function
    def tearDownClass(cls):
        clear_tabs()
        # only available in 2.7, solves the above issue
        if not BaseTest.isPythonVersion2_6:
            cls.logger.note('tearDownClass')
            BaseTest.delete()

    @classmethod
    @log.test_class_function
    def __del__(self):
        clear_tabs()
        # this method causes issues in 2.7, the logger gets deleted first
        if BaseTest.isPythonVersion2_6:
            self.logger.note('__del__')
            BaseTest.delete()

    @log.test_class_function
    def _configure(self):
        clear_tabs()
        Controller.configure()

    @log.test_class_function
    def _delete(self):
        clear_tabs()
        if BaseTest.deleteCalled:
            self.logger.info('Delete already performed, exiting...')
            return
        BaseTest.deleteCalled = True
        self.logger.note('Cleanup phase')
        Events().ee.emit("terminate", self)
        self.cleanup()
        Controller.cleanup()
        cleanupEmptyDirectories(self.config)
        printFooter(self.name, self.testDirectory,
                    self.startTime, self.currentResult, self.logger)
        Events().ee.emit("at_end")
        createColorlessLog(self.testDirectory)
        createHtmlLog(self.testDirectory)

    def _calcTestFileInformation(self, testFile):
        testFile = os.path.splitext(testFile)[0]
        if testFile.startswith('./'):
            testFile = testFile[2:]
        testName = testFile.split('/')
        self.name = testName[-1]
        self.path = testFile

    def _calcTestDirectory(self):
        self.testDirectory = os.path.join(
                self.config.framework.resultsPath,
                os.path.splitext(self.path)[0])
        if not os.path.exists(self.testDirectory):
            os.makedirs(self.testDirectory)
        makeDirectories(self.name, self.testDirectory, self.config)

    def _fixPython3SslIssue(self):
        try:
            ssl._create_default_https_context = ssl._create_unverified_context
        except Exception, e:
            self.logger.warning('Exception:%s' % str(e))

blocks.title: GeminiFramework.pdf
blocks.content:
Software Architecture Document

        HBR-QA Team

        Kevin Guerra

         Version 1.0

          June 2015

                                          Revision History

NOTE: The revision history cycle begins once changes or enhancements are requested after the initial
      version of the Software Architecture Document has been completed.

    Date            Version      Description                      Author
    06/12/2015      1.0          Initial version                  Kevin Guerra

                                            Table of Contents

1. Introduction       1
    1.1. Purpose        1
    1.2. Scope1
    1.3. Definitions, Acronyms, and Abbreviations    1
    1.4. References 2
    1.5. Overview       2
2. Concept    2
3. Layers     2
    3.1. Base, Framework Layer or Architectural Layer 3
    3.2. High Level  4
    3.3. Middle      4
    3.4. Low Level   5
4. Structure 5
5. Initialization     5
6. Test Framework     5
7. Error Handling     6
8. Sample Test        6
9. Development        6

                                  HBR-QA Testing Framework

1. Introduction
This document provides a high level overview and explains the main classes and interactions for a
new framework and architecture for HBR-QA Testing. It could serve as an evolving document. It
explains how a test developer will be able to use the system, how the system operates internally,
including interactions with other libraries and frameworks, specifically vmtree.

NOTE: Development is at an early stage, therefore this document is subject to change.

   1.1.     Purpose
   To provide a high level overview of the new framework, information on its usage and explain the
   similarities and differences with the current framework, so that developers who are already
   familiar with the previous framework would be better able to migrate.

   1.2.      Scope
   Initially the scope is moderate, since this system is at the early development stage. It should
   explain the architectural layers, system initialization, output, configuration, object interactions,
   error handling, and main concepts.

   There is much information that stems from the vmtree library, this framework will serve as a kind
   of wrapper that attempts to group functionality in a way that feels more natural, and an
   explanation on that will be provided. However, the internals on how those libraries work, are
   better detailed in their respective documentation. Therefore, only minimal information may be
   provided about them.

   1.3.    Definitions, Acronyms, and Abbreviations
   The system will avoid abbreviations as much as possible, and only use those that the team
   members are accustomed to. In the new framework it will be necessary to change some terms
   in order to better model the system. This may cause some confusion at first, due to people
   being used to certain terminology. Nevertheless, the benefit of having a more accurate model
   far outweigh the disadvantages of having to relearn the terms, specially for those who are
   unfamiliar.
           HBR – Host Based Replication
           HMS - Host Management Server
           VC – Virtual Center
           ESX – Elastic Sky X ('The' Virtualization Server)
           VM – Virtual Machine
           Layer 1 – High Level Layer, aka Module
           Layer 2 – Middle Layer, aka Object
           Layer 3 – Low Level Layer, aka Support
           Base Layer – Framework or Architectural Layer
   Other terms may pop up, related to libraries, technologies, etc..., explanation on those is beyond
   scope.

   1.4.  References
        vmtree library

   1.5.     Overview

   In order to fully document the aspects of the framework, the Software Architecture Document
   contains the following subsections.
   Concept: Describes the system conceptually and its operation
   Layers: Description about the multi-tier architecture, and how interactions happen
   Initialization: How the framework, test and objects are configured, initialized and disposed of
   Test Framework: Describes how classes from which a test object would derive
   Error Handling: Best practices for error handling
   Sample Test: A sample test scenario that describes the system in full operation.
   Development: Methodologies used, approach.

2. Concept
    A test should interact with the system at the highest level possible
    A test configuration should contain if possible all data, so that the system can build the
     necessary objects, automatically
    When the system initializes, the test should already have all objects it needs
    The objects are initialized but not yet connected, unless specified in the configuration or
     through the framework
    Extra objects needed or configuration changes should be done via the framework API, or
     more precisely Layer 1 (High Level Layer)
    The framework is responsible for maintaining state and reporting results
    The test should follow a linear execution path as it currently does, thus avoiding threading
     issues and other complications

3. Layers
The layers contain classes which are instantiated as objects. Sometimes is challenging deciding in
which layer an object should go. So the criteria we'll use is the following:

    Base: utilities, helpers, anything that is useful to all layers and test
    Low: Mostly stateless, and many will not be objects but functions. For example, parsing, file
     path handling, invoking certain functionality from libraries, where in those you pass in a
     context object, since the low level object is passed in a context, it need not keep state
     locally, more specifically, for instance, you invoke dd command from some host, however,
     you would not invoke the command from the middle layer directly, but an API should be
     available, so you would pass in an ssh connection object to the function and would run a
     high level command, say 'WriteBytes' done to a disk object, you don't care how it's done, but
     the low level function knows that it needs to invoke dd on the host of the connection you
     passed in.
    Middle: Objects that keep some state, but conceptually are of lower level, but support the
     higher layer. For example a connection class, state is necessary, but it is not suitable for a
     test to call directly into it, because the concern of a connection is of lower grade, IOW, a test
     should not have to be concerned about connectivity issues, such as initiating directly, etc...

        High: Objects here keep all the state provided by the configuration assigned at initialization.
         Ideally, tests only interact with this layer.

STATE
    Object state is encapsulated and managed internally. Data are not accessible outside, use
      proper API
    In the object hierarchy, state changes may affect contained objects, even parent objects
      (hopefully not.) Containers will use proper channels for effecting said changes
    Class data is part of state, for example fields, collections, etc...

PARAMETERS
   Parameter usage is minimized using a layered approach. Please see below for details
   Functions with one or two parameters can use positional parameters
   Functions with more, should use named parameters
   Where applicable, parameters can be passed as dictionaries, this is very common in python
   Sanitation (cleaning up, removing extra characters from strings, etc…,) verification (checking for
     the existence of a parameter,) validation, should be done preferably after init and in a module
     responsible for such tasks. The problem here is that, the module ends up ‘knowing’ too much
     about the system as a whole. However, the advantage is too good to pass up, and I’ve had very
     good success rates with this approach. The end result being that your code base ends up very
     uncluttered from these concerns. One way to think about this is akin to entry points to a
     function, where all of these are done. However in this approach, these tasks are done in one
     module. So, when you are working on your function, you make sure that all these preconditions
     are met in the proper module. However, because many function group naturally, the result is
     that the work done for one function should be applicable to the rest of the group. How does this
     work in practice? A simple example would be host configuration (host name, user, password.)
     During init, those values are checked, for example, that they exist, and that the data appears to
     make sense, then, when you actually call ssh connect and pass in the parameters, the function
     would not have to recheck them. This provides a good separation of concern about data
     validation, verification, etc… from its utilization.
   3.1. Base, Framework Layer or Architectural Layer

           Configuration
            ◦ Framework to build objects from configuration at initialization
            ◦ Configuration file per test would allow for tailored setups, main configuration to
               contain 'Lego' blocks
            ◦ Built objects are not to be initialized (meaning, creation of VMware objects or
               connections), unless a configuration parameter explicitly states so
            ◦ Extra objects, override parameters, to be also processed at initialization, with the
               same effects as above
            ◦ TO AVOID the HUGE MESS of configuration data: Configuration data only to be
               used at initialization, at which point the proper objects take on or appropriate the
               values, therefore, changing configuration data after initialization should have ZERO
               side effects
            ◦ Configuration data are not state data, Configuration data are not state data,
               Configuration data are not state data... etc...
            ◦ Configuration data are not parameters, Configuration data are not parameters,
               Configuration data are not parameters... etc...
            ◦ The two eloquently put points above avoid a lot of misunderstandings about what's
               happening in the code, this is major
            ◦ Configuration data should be fairly stable, that is, it should resemble the objects
               capabilities, and only change to reflect changes, additions or removal of capabilities,
               this implies that a good location to put extra data, would not be configuration data

         ◦ State data is the responsibility of each object and should not be made available
            directly, only through proper APIs, ideally the objects should use FSM (Finite State
            Machine) where applicable
         ◦ We should never treat an object as another via injecting configuration data, as it is
            done now. A properly constructed class instance should be fairly stable. If you need
            an object of the same type, but with different configuration, for the love of Pete, just
            create another object, there is immense confusion about how to interpret object
            interactions because of this
         ◦ Because of how we keep proper configuration data, parameter passing should be
            minimized. This alone would result in a reduction of code of about 40%, it’s hard to
            believe, but notice that in the current code base about that percentage is dedicated
            to handling of configuration/parameter/state data, along with verification, parsing,
            etc… which happens at various stages for the same data!
         ◦ After initialization, all objects have been initialized with their proper data, at this point
            the Configuration object may remain, but its use should be extremely rare, if at all.
            The passing of data between objects should be done through the proper API, we
            should never use sneaky ways to pass data around or even return values. Python
            can return tuples or even dictionaries. The runtime overhead is negligible comparing
            with the huge cognitive overhead of sneaky data passing
        Logger
         ◦ Automatic logging for functions
         ◦ Colorized
        Report

3.2. High Level
     Actors: Configured objects, will contain all objects listed in configuration
     Group: Hbr group, it may morph into something that will manage objects more naturally, or it
      could simply expand its capacity, so that, if you group objects, you may call high level
      functions on the group and it would affect the group members where applicable
     Esx
     Hbr
     Vc
     Vm
     Replica

3.3. Middle
     Device
     Disk
     Nic
     Connection
     Command?

3.4. Low Level
Layer 3 must do the heavy lifting, path cleaning, parsing, etc... because these should be low grade
concerns and we want to avoid cluttering the higher layers and specially the tests
     SshConnection: used in vmtree and gemini
     SoapConnection: used in vmtree
     Staf
     Spaz
     RemoteCommand

      LocalCommand

4. Structure
Directory structure and file organization
Gemini
  -Suite
   -FunctionalGroup1, etc...: Test1, Test2, etc...
   CheckConfiguration: Check server directories, configs, etc... explicit call, not done for all tests,
time consuming
  -Test
  -Lib: Log, Configuration, Report,
   -Module: Actors, Group, Esx, Hbr, Machine, Replica,
     -Object: Device, Disk, Nic, Connection, Command,
      -Support: SshConnection, SoapConnection, Staf, Spaz, RemoteCommand, LocalCommand,
   -Setup: Factories, Configure, Initialize, Verify, Cleanup

5. Initialization
      User runs a test from the command line
      Configuration files are read:
       ◦ Global configuration: contains configuration blocks, and 'default' virtual system, for
          example: ESX, Virtual machines, Replicas and such should not be here
       ◦ Test configuration: if exists, contains extra blocks, other machines, etc... data on which
          machines to startup
       ◦ User configuration: contains specific user configuration, such as IP addresses, host
          names, etc... the values here replace those in the other configuration files, IOW, override
      Objects get created from configuration and added hierarchically in Actors
      Automatic commands in configuration are run, such as connect to ESX, create VMs, etc...

6.   Test Framework
      After initialization, the test code can call high level commands, with reasonable expectations
       that the 'right' things will happen:
       ◦ Error reporting, to log and if applicable to Report
       ◦ When reasonable, error recovery, for instance if an ssh connection is broken, it can be
           retried a max_num of times
      Derives from Python UnitTest, so it's predictible, we'll use the standard assert facilities

7. Error Handling
 Because this is a testing framework, a proper error handling strategy is integral, however, the
  aim should be to handle it consistently and ideally automatically by the Layer 1 classes
 Typically in most API frameworks, error handling responsibility is delegated to the caller or
  client. However, I believe in a testing framework, the framework itself is not as much an API as it
  is an actual system. Therefore, this responsibility would be more appropriately centralized and
  handled in the framework, for the most part.
 The tests should have some asserts and verification lines, however, whenever possible these
  should happen in Layer 1

    This would have the effect that the test code would be very minimalistic, since having a good
     separation of concerns is paramount to good OOP. For example, if you call a Layer 1 function to
     say VM.PowerOn(), it should not be the responsibility of the test to make sure it happened, but
     of the Layer 1 object, moreover, the framework should also handle the error
    For the reason above, the error handling for that function should of course be in Layer 1. The
     effect would be that when an error occurs, by default they would abort the test, as the current
     framework does now. However, flexibility should be built so that when desirable, some methods
     would report an error but not abort the test, so it should be up to the test developer to opt out of
     the default
    Exception handling seems to be quite adequate for error handling in most cases, however, we
     should minimize the proliferation of derived exception classes. The best practice is to use what
     the language and system provide, and only create a new class when its use would offer
     additional functionality other than just print out the error. In other words, when we want to catch
     a very specific type of error because we will handle it in a non-standard way (more than print.) A
     good example would be for an SshConnection object. Let’s say that a connection gets broken,
     an operation is sent through the broken pipe, bam! Error, we would have a notification method
     that would get called in order to re-establish the connection. Or not, simply abort the test, since
     a broken connection is usually an indication of something bad not usually related to our test.
     This is a good illustration of why extra exception classes are really, exceptional

   8. Sample Test
       

   9. Development
PROGRAMMING STYLE
   OOP: I’ll attempt to follow most/all OOP principles, unless impractical. I like the SOLID
    guidelines:
            Single responsibility principle: A class should have only a single responsibility. This one
             is hard to reason about in the higher layers, but should apply conceptually
            Open/closed principle: Open for extension, closed for modification. This can be achieved
             but only completely after the framework is done. However, because of layering, changes
             on lower layers shouldn

… [truncated for display]

segments.content:
Software Architecture Document

        HBR-QA Team

        Kevin Guerra

         Version 1.0

          June 2015

Revision History

NOTE: The revision history cycle begins once changes or enhancements are requested after the initial
      version of the Software Architecture Document has been completed.

    Date            Version      Description                      Author
    06/12/2015      1.0          Initial version                  Kevin Guerra

Table of Contents

1. Introduction       1
    1.1. Purpose        1
    1.2. Scope1
    1.3. Definitions, Acronyms, and Abbreviations    1
    1.4. References 2
    1.5. Overview       2
2. Concept    2
3. Layers     2
    3.1. Base, Framework Layer or Architectural Layer 3
    3.2. High Level  4
    3.3. Middle      4
    3.4. Low Level   5
4. Structure 5
5. Initialization     5
6. Test Framework     5
7. Error Handling     6
8. Sample Test        6
9. Development        6

HBR-QA Testing Framework

1. Introduction
This document provides a high level overview and explains the main classes and interactions for a
new framework and architecture for HBR-QA Testing. It could serve as an evolving document. It
explains how a test developer will be able to use the system, how the system operates internally,
including interactions with other libraries and frameworks, specifically vmtree.

NOTE: Development is at an early stage, therefore this document is subject to change.

   1.1.     Purpose
   To provide a high level overview of the new framework, information on its usage and explain the
   similarities and differences with the current framework, so that developers who are already
   familiar with the previous framework would be better able to migrate.

   1.2.      Scope
   Initially the scope is moderate, since this system is at the early development stage. It should
   explain the architectural layers, system initialization, output, configuration, object interactions,
   error handling, and main concepts.

   There is much information that stems from the vmtree library, this framework will serve as a kind
   of wrapper that attempts to group functionality in a way that feels more natural, and an
   explanation on that will be provided. However, the internals on how those libraries work, are
   better detailed in their respective documentation. Therefore, only minimal information may be
   provided about them.

   1.3.    Definitions, Acronyms, and Abbreviations
   The system will avoid abbreviations as much as possible, and only use those that the team
   members are accustomed to. In the new framework it will be necessary to change some terms
   in order to better model the system. This may cause some confusion at first, due to people
   being used to certain terminology. Nevertheless, the benefit of having a more accurate model
   far outweigh the disadvantages of having to relearn the terms, specially for those who are
   unfamiliar.
           HBR – Host Based Replication
           HMS - Host Management Server
           VC – Virtual Center
           ESX – Elastic Sky X ('The' Virtualization Server)
           VM – Virtual Machine
           Layer 1 – High Level Layer, aka Module
           Layer 2 – Middle Layer, aka Object
           Layer 3 – Low Level Layer, aka Support
           Base Layer – Framework or Architectural Layer
   Other terms may pop up, related to libraries, technologies, etc..., explanation on those is beyond
   scope.

1.4.  References
        vmtree library

   1.5.     Overview

   In order to fully document the aspects of the framework, the Software Architecture Document
   contains the following subsections.
   Concept: Describes the system conceptually and its operation
   Layers: Description about the multi-tier architecture, and how interactions happen
   Initialization: How the framework, test and objects are configured, initialized and disposed of
   Test Framework: Describes how classes from which a test object would derive
   Error Handling: Best practices for error handling
   Sample Test: A sample test scenario that describes the system in full operation.
   Development: Methodologies used, approach.

2. Concept
    A test should interact with the system at the highest level possible
    A test configuration should contain if possible all data, so that the system can build the
     necessary objects, automatically
    When the system initializes, the test should already have all objects it needs
    The objects are initialized but not yet connected, unless specified in the configuration or
     through the framework
    Extra objects needed or configuration changes should be done via the framework API, or
     more precisely Layer 1 (High Level Layer)
    The framework is responsible for maintaining state and reporting results
    The test should follow a linear execution path as it currently does, thus avoiding threading
     issues and other complications

3. Layers
The layers contain classes which are instantiated as objects. Sometimes is challenging deciding in
which layer an object should go. So the criteria we'll use is the following:

    Base: utilities, helpers, anything that is useful to all layers and test
    Low: Mostly stateless, and many will not be objects but functions. For example, parsing, file
     path handling, invoking certain functionality from libraries, where in those you pass in a
     context object, since the low level object is passed in a context, it need not keep state
     locally, more specifically, for instance, you invoke dd command from some host, however,
     you would not invoke the command from the middle layer directly, but an API should be
     available, so you would pass in an ssh connection object to the function and would run a
     high level command, say 'WriteBytes' done to a disk object, you don't care how it's done, but
     the low level function knows that it needs to invoke dd on the host of the connection you
     passed in.
    Middle: Objects that keep some state, but conceptually are of lower level, but support the
     higher layer. For example a connection class, state is necessary, but it is not suitable for a
     test to call directly into it, because the concern of a connection is of lower grade, IOW, a test
     should not have to be concerned about connectivity issues, such as initiating directly, etc...

 High: Objects here keep all the state provided by the configuration assigned at initialization.
         Ideally, tests only interact with this layer.

STATE
    Object state is encapsulated and managed internally. Data are not accessible outside, use
      proper API
    In the object hierarchy, state changes may affect contained objects, even parent objects
      (hopefully not.) Containers will use proper channels for effecting said changes
    Class data is part of state, for example fields, collections, etc...

PARAMETERS
   Parameter usage is minimized using a layered approach. Please see below for details
   Functions with one or two parameters can use positional parameters
   Functions with more, should use named parameters
   Where applicable, parameters can be passed as dictionaries, this is very common in python
   Sanitation (cleaning up, removing extra characters from strings, etc…,) verification (checking for
     the existence of a parameter,) validation, should be done preferably after init and in a module
     responsible for such tasks. The problem here is that, the module ends up ‘knowing’ too much
     about the system as a whole. However, the advantage is too good to pass up, and I’ve had very
     good success rates with this approach. The end result being that your code base ends up very
     uncluttered from these concerns. One way to think about this is akin to entry points to a
     function, where all of these are done. However in this approach, these tasks are done in one
     module. So, when you are working on your function, you make sure that all these preconditions
     are met in the proper module. However, because many function group naturally, the result is
     that the work done for one function should be applicable to the rest of the group. How does this
     work in practice? A simple example would be host configuration (host name, user, password.)
     During init, those values are checked, for example, that they exist, and that the data appears to
     make sense, then, when you actually call ssh connect and pass in the parameters, the function
     would not have to recheck them. This provides a good separation of concern about data
     validation, verification, etc… from its utilization.
   3.1. Base, Framework Layer or Architectural Layer

           Configuration
            ◦ Framework to build objects from configuration at initialization
            ◦ Configuration file per test would allow for tailored setups, main configuration to
               contain 'Lego' blocks
            ◦ Built objects are not to be initialized (meaning, creation of VMware objects or
               connections), unless a configuration parameter explicitly states so
            ◦ Extra objects, override parameters, to be also processed at initialization, with the
               same effects as above
            ◦ TO AVOID the HUGE MESS of configuration data: Configuration data only to be
               used at initialization, at which point the proper objects take on or appropriate the
               values, therefore, changing configuration data after initialization should have ZERO
               side effects
            ◦ Configuration data are not state data, Configuration data are not state data,
               Configuration data are not state data... etc...
            ◦ Configuration data are not parameters, Configuration data are not parameters,
               Configuration data are not parameters... etc...
            ◦ The two eloquently put points above avoid a lot of misunderstandings about what's
               happening in the code, this is major
            ◦ Configuration data should be fairly stable, that is, it should resemble the objects
               capabilities, and only change to reflect changes, additions or removal of capabilities,
               this implies that a good location to put extra data, would not be configuration data

◦ State data is the responsibility of each object and should not be made available
            directly, only through proper APIs, ideally the objects should use FSM (Finite State
            Machine) where applicable
         ◦ We should never treat an object as another via injecting configuration data, as it is
            done now. A properly constructed class instance should be fairly stable. If you need
            an object of the same type, but with different configuration, for the love of Pete, just
            create another object, there is immense confusion about how to interpret object
            interactions because of this
         ◦ Because of how we keep proper configuration data, parameter passing should be
            minimized. This alone would result in a reduction of code of about 40%, it’s hard to
            believe, but notice that in the current code base about that percentage is dedicated
            to handling of configuration/parameter/state data, along with verification, parsing,
            etc… which happens at various stages for the same data!
         ◦ After initialization, all objects have been initialized with their proper data, at this point
            the Configuration object may remain, but its use should be extremely rare, if at all.
            The passing of data between objects should be done through the proper API, we
            should never use sneaky ways to pass data around or even return values. Python
            can return tuples or even dictionaries. The runtime overhead is negligible comparing
            with the huge cognitive overhead of sneaky data passing
        Logger
         ◦ Automatic logging for functions
         ◦ Colorized
        Report

3.2. High Level
     Actors: Configured objects, will contain all objects listed in configuration
     Group: Hbr group, it may morph into something that will manage objects more naturally, or it
      could simply expand its capacity, so that, if you group objects, you may call high level
      functions on the group and it would affect the group members where applicable
     Esx
     Hbr
     Vc
     Vm
     Replica

3.3. Middle
     Device
     Disk
     Nic
     Connection
     Command?

3.4. Low Level
Layer 3 must do the heavy lifting, path cleaning, parsing, etc... because these should be low grade
concerns and we want to avoid cluttering the higher layers and specially the tests
     SshConnection: used in vmtree and gemini
     SoapConnection: used in vmtree
     Staf
     Spaz
     RemoteCommand

 LocalCommand

4. Structure
Directory structure and file organization
Gemini
  -Suite
   -FunctionalGroup1, etc...: Test1, Test2, etc...
   CheckConfiguration: Check server directories, configs, etc... explicit call, not done for all tests,
time consuming
  -Test
  -Lib: Log, Configuration, Report,
   -Module: Actors, Group, Esx, Hbr, Machine, Replica,
     -Object: Device, Disk, Nic, Connection, Command,
      -Support: SshConnection, SoapConnection, Staf, Spaz, RemoteCommand, LocalCommand,
   -Setup: Factories, Configure, Initialize, Verify, Cleanup

5. Initialization
      User runs a test from the command line
      Configuration files are read:
       ◦ Global configuration: contains configuration blocks, and 'default' virtual system, for
          example: ESX, Virtual machines, Replicas and such should not be here
       ◦ Test configuration: if exists, contains extra blocks, other machines, etc... data on which
          machines to startup
       ◦ User configuration: contains specific user configuration, such as IP addresses, host
          names, etc... the values here replace those in the other configuration files, IOW, override
      Objects get created from configuration and added hierarchically in Actors
      Automatic commands in configuration are run, such as connect to ESX, create VMs, etc...

6.   Test Framework
      After initialization, the test code can call high level commands, with reasonable expectations
       that the 'right' things will happen:
       ◦ Error reporting, to log and if applicable to Report
       ◦ When reasonable, error recovery, for instance if an ssh connection is broken, it can be
           retried a max_num of times
      Derives from Python UnitTest, so it's predictible, we'll use the standard assert facilities

7. Error Handling
 Because this is a testing framework, a proper error handling strategy is integral, however, the
  aim should be to handle it consistently and ideally automatically by the Layer 1 classes
 Typically in most API frameworks, error handling responsibility is delegated to the caller or
  client. However, I believe in a testing framework, the framework itself is not as much an API as it
  is an actual system. Therefore, this responsibility would be more appropriately centralized and
  handled in the framework, for the most part.
 The tests should have some asserts and verification lines, however, whenever possible these
  should happen in Layer 1

 This would have the effect that the test code would be very minimalistic, since having a good
     separation of concerns is paramount to good OOP. For example, if you call a Layer 1 function to
     say VM.PowerOn(), it should not be the responsibility of the test to make sure it happened, but
     of the Layer 1 object, moreover, the framework should also handle the error
    For the reason above, the error handling for that function should of course be in Layer 1. The
     effect would be that when an error occurs, by default they would abort the test, as the current
     framework does now. However, flexibility should be built so that when desirable, some methods
     would report an error but not abort the test, so it should be up to the test developer to opt out of
     the default
    Exception handling seems to be quite adequate for error handling in most cases, however, we
     should minimize the proliferation of derived exception classes. The best practice is to use what
     the language and system provide, and only create a new class when its use would offer
     additional functionality other than just print out the error. In other words, when we want to catch
     a very specific type of error because we will handle it in a non-standard way (more than print.) A
     good example would be for an SshConnection object. Let’s say that a connection gets broken,
     an operation is sent through the broken pipe, bam! Error, we would have a notification method
     that would get called in order to re-establish the connection. Or not, simply abort the test, since
     a broken connection is usually an indication of something bad not usually related to our test.
     This is a good illustration of why extra exception classes are really, exceptional

   8. Sample Test
       

   9. Development
PROGRAMMING STYLE
   OOP: I’ll attempt to follow most/all OOP principles, unless impractical. I like the SOLID
    guidelines:
            Single responsibility principle: A class should have only a single responsibility. This one
             is hard to reason about in the higher layers, but should apply conceptually
            Open/closed principle: Open for extension, closed for modification. This can be achieved
             but only completely after the framework is done. However, because of layering, changes
             on lower layers shouldn’t disturb the tests so much.
            Liskov substitution principle: Polymorphic behavior, permits use of mock objects
            Interface segregation principle: Currently we have the opposite of this. We have several
             god classes. Some people opine that OO code is hard to read, because of the levels of
             indirection in objects calling other objects. This is compounded by this principle because
             ideally you have more and smaller objects that interact. I think that the advantages far
             outweigh the disadvantages. This, in and of itself allows for good code orthogonality,
             which allows you to do more with less code, and the less code you have, the easier it is
             to understand. This does not mean that one can just look at a framework and just
             understand it right away. There is a learning curve as with anything of relative
             complexity. So practice makes perfect.
            Dependency inversion principle: This one is a bit clunky to do in Python, but it’s doable.
             This would give us the ability to inject mock objects to the factory classes, so that we can
             tests our tests while we develop. We should prepare to say good bye to having to run
             the full test to test our newly written or changed code. We can also do negative testing
             with mock objects. For example, you can pass in a parameter, or whatever to cause the
             mock object to produce an error, and we would see that our test can react properly.

    Functional Programming: I’ll attempt to code methods with no variables or state where
    applicable. This has some advantages in code understandability and is less error prone. Some
    code should be just a function. People get caught up in the conundrum Java created when they
    sort of dictated everything should be a class. Well, not so at all. Some concepts map naturally to
    functions.
   Simplicity over cleverness: Self explanatory
   YAGNI: Keep things as simple as possible and only add code when needed, however, I’ll try to
    keep some symmetry, for example, if you have a create, put a delete, even if at the moment is
    not needed and adding it would not take too long
   Orthogonal Design: DRY principle, good refactoring, avoiding duplication of code, composition
   Naming Convention: This is, I think a very important part
         Eliminate abbreviations, because they usually cause ambiguity, are used inconsistently
            and make the code very ugly. With exceptions for VMWare objects: VM, ESX, HBR, and
            may be a few others. However, if we use these, when choosing method names, they
            would morph into Vm, Esx, Hbr, etc…same for variable names. On second thought I
            think the V in Vm is superfluous because we only deal with virtual machines. So I think
            just the word Machine, and then you can inherit from that ProtectedMachine and
            ReplicaMachine, or rather ReplicatedMachine, protected is asymmetrical, because one
            is Replicated and the other a Replica, yes it is by implication ‘protected’ but in reality it is
            more of a replica source. Sorry, I have to work on the name or maybe protected should
            suffice.
         Eliminate superfluous prefixes and suffixes. They do not convey any information and
            have the effect that they make you pause when reading the code. For example, the
            libraries of the form HbrApiFileName.py: We already know it’s an API, we already know
            it’s about HBR because the parent directory is named to allude to that fact. Keeping
            simpler names makes it easier to ‘guess’ what module or method one needs, when one
            is not familiar enough with the code.
         Names like Helper, Utility, Manager, Module, Framework, Api, those aren’t very useful.
            They’re at least bad style.
         Careful and consistent selection of words. If we choose delete() all should be named
            that, not remove(), erase(), etc…
         Creating a coding or naming convention is beyond scope at this point, but there are a
            few out there in common use: PEP, Google, Pythonista. I’m not very picky as to what we
            use (except for names) but we should all agree on something we can all live with.
            Question: Do we have a python coding standard at VMWare?
   Decorators: Python decorators are very handy because they allow for good separation of
    concerns. I’m already using them for logging, at function start and exit. Planning on using them
    for parameter/input validation in applicable cases.

@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@

                                    * * *   N E W   A R C H I T E C T U R E   * * *
@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@

========================== 
OBJECT HIERARCHY
========================== 

Actors: Object conglomerate
  - hierarchical 
  - flat, for some types: disks=all disks, replicaDisks, etc...
  
= Inheritance
+ Composition
- Attribute
 
 spaz staf ssh
 
========================== 
OBJECTS      
========================== 

CLASSES
  Esx 
  Device
    = Disk
    = Nic 
  Machine
    + Hbr
    + Protected
    + Replica
    - Device
    - Snapshot
    
FRAMEWORK
  Test = TestCase
    ***USER_TEST***
    + Actors
    + Workflow
    + Configuration
    + Utility: logger
    
WORKFLOW
 - purpose: isolate, reuse, compliance
 - modules: vmtree modules
 - objects: vmtree objects
 - factories: object factories
 - container: borg/singleton object container
 - snippets
   - perform atomic specific, low granularity code, from modules
            
========================== 
STATE
========================== 

MACHINE:
  - Invalid
  - PoweredOff
  - PoweredOn
  - Suspended

DISK:
  - Invalid
  - Detached
  - Attached

-------------------------- 
ATTRIBUTES
-------------------------- 

MACHINE:
  - Protected
  - Replica
  - Spaz?

DISK:
  - Protected
  - Replica

========================== 
SYSTEM OPERATION
========================== 
 - configuration
   - check results dirs, files, etc...
   - read yaml
     - concat configs, output to Results dir, load, replace env tokens, output to compiled 
   - create objects (inject dependencies)
   - create workflow container
   - create actors container
   - call preInit: user can create or configure objects, discouraged
 - initialization
   - save system state
   - init: connections, start machines, create disks, replicas, etc...
   - call postInit: user can check conditions, if necessary, discouraged
   - yield to TEST
 - cleanup
   - call preCleanup, discouraged
   - power off, delete, etc... based on previous system state
 - report
      
========================== 
FILES
========================== 
+ dir 
- file

RESULTS DIR STRUCTURE and FILES
 + /tmp/results
 + data: certificates, etc...
 + test: name of test
   - configuration.yaml: compiled full configuration
   - objects.yaml: high level objects, created during configuration
   - log.txt, log.html
   - report.txt, report.html
 + sut: system under test
   + pre + post + diff + runtime
     - files list
     - system logs
     - system stats: disk utilization, processes, memory, etc...
 + tmp
      
      
========================== 
========================== 

CONFIGURATION

blocks.title: TestHostRemove.py
blocks.content:
#!/usr/bin/python
import unittest
from lib.utility import log
from lib.base_test import BaseTest

@log.class_logger
class TestHostRemove(BaseTest):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        BaseTest.__init__(self, __file__, *args, **kwargs)

    @log.test_class_function
    def test_HostRemove(self):
        self.logger.test('Hbr: %s' % self.Hbr)
        self.logger.test('esxs: %s' % str(self.Esxs))

        esxToBeRemoved = self.Esxs[0]
        self.logger.test('esxToBeRemoved: %s' % str(esxToBeRemoved))
        self.assertTrue(self.Hosts.exist(esxToBeRemoved),
                        'Host %s does not exist' % esxToBeRemoved.ip)

        hostCountBefore = self.Hosts.count()
        self.logger.test('hostCountBefore: %d' % hostCountBefore)

        self.Hosts.remove(esxToBeRemoved)

        hostCountAfter = self.Hosts.count()
        self.logger.test('hostCountAfter: %d' % hostCountAfter)
        self.logger.test('esxs: %s' % str(self.Esxs))

        self.assertEqual(hostCountBefore - 1, hostCountAfter,
                         'Total number of hosts should have decreased '
                         'by one, number of hosts changed from %d to %d'
                         % (hostCountBefore, hostCountAfter))

        self.assertFalse(self.Hosts.exist(esxToBeRemoved),
                         'After removing, host %s, it should not exist'
                         % esxToBeRemoved.ip)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    unittest.main()

blocks.title: esx.py
blocks.content:
from . import *
from lib.utility.dict_as_member import DottedNotation
from helper.identity_generator import generateOpId
from lib.service.ssh import Ssh
from lib.service.remote_log_reader import RemoteLogReader
from lib.utility.progress_bar import progress_bar_init
from lib.utility.progress_bar import progress_task
import pyVim
from pyVmomi import Vim
from pyHbr import task
from pyHbr import hostd
from pyVmomi import Vmodl
import pyHbr

@log.class_logger
@pretty_print
class Esxs(Actors):

    def __init__(self, items):
        self.initialize(items)
        for item in items:
            if item.enabled:
                self.append(Esx.build(item))
            else:
                self.append(NullObject.build(item))

    def remove(self, item):
        Actors.remove(self, item)

@log.class_logger
@pretty_print
class Esx(Actor):

    @staticmethod
    def build(esx):
        return Esx(esx)

    def __init__(self, esx):
        self.initialize(esx)
        self._hbr = None
        self.host = None
        self.HostDatastore = None
        self.HostDevice = None
        self.HostMachine = None
        self.HostReplication = None
        self.HostSnapshot = None
        self.HostTask = None
        self.HostDisk = None
        self.Host = None
        self.RemoteFile = None
        self.PsfFile = None
        self.fullPath = None
        self.serviceInstance = None
        self.credentials = DottedNotation(self.credentials)
        self.datastore = DottedNotation(self.datastore)
        self.tools = DottedNotation(self.tools)
        self.authenticationString = \
            getAuthenticationString(self.credentials.user,
                                   self.credentials.password,
                                   self.ip)

    def _setHbr(self, hbr):
        self._hbr = hbr

    @log.low_class_function
    def configure(self):
        self._ssh = Ssh(self.ip,
                       self.credentials.user,
                       self.credentials.password,
                       self.logger.esx,
                       self.logger.esxLog)
        self.logger.info('logs:%s' % (self.logs))
        self.filesDirectory = '%s/%s' % (self.filesDirectory, self.name)
        command = "/bin/mkdir -vp %s" % self.filesDirectory
        (code, stdout) = runCommand(command)
        self.logsDirectory = '%s/%s' % (self.logsDirectory, self.name)
        command = "/bin/mkdir -vp %s" % self.logsDirectory
        (code, stdout) = runCommand(command)
        self._remoteLogReader = RemoteLogReader(self._ssh, 
                                                self.logsDirectory,
                                                logFiles=self.logs,
                                                color=log.GREEN35)
        self.host = pyHbr.hostd.Hostd(self.ip,
                                self.credentials.user,
                                self.credentials.password)
        self.logger.info('host:%s' % (self.host))
        self.hostServiceInstance = self.host.GetServiceInstance()
        self.logger.info('hostServiceInstance:%s' % (self.hostServiceInstance))
        self.authorizationManager = self.host.GetAuthorizationManager()
        self.logger.info('authorizationManager:%s'
                         % (self.authorizationManager))
        self.logger.info('self.datastore.name:%s'
                         % (self.datastore.name))
        self.remoteStorage = self.host.RemoteStorage(self.datastore.name)
        self.logger.info('remoteStorage:%s' % (self.remoteStorage))
        self.serviceInstance = self.host.GetServiceInstance()
        self.logger.info('serviceInstance:%s' % (self.serviceInstance))
        content = self.serviceInstance.RetrieveContent()
        datacenters = content.GetRootFolder().GetChildEntity()
        self.datacenter = datacenters[0]
        self.logger.info('datacenter:%s' % (self.datacenter))
        self.HostDatastore.configure()
        self.HostDevice.configure()
        self.HostMachine.configure()
        self.HostReplication.configure()
        self.HostSnapshot.configure()
        self.HostTask.configure()
        self.HostDisk.configure()
        self.Host.configure()
        self.RemoteFile.configure()
        self.PsfFile.configure()
        self.logger.debug('configured Esx:%s' % self)

    @log.low_class_function
    def check(self):
        # TODO, check for this
        # datastore.accessible
        self.logger.debug('self.tools:%s' % (self.tools))
        command = "ls %s" % self.tools.disk_tool
        run_ssh(self._ssh, command)

        command = "ls %s" % self.tools.disk_create
        run_ssh(self._ssh, command)

        command = ('vsish -e get /system/modules/hbr_filter/general'
                   ' | grep version:')
        run_ssh(self._ssh, command)

        command = "esxcli storage filesystem list -i"
        run_ssh(self._ssh, command)

        command = "readlink -f '/vmfs/volumes/datastore1/'"
        run_ssh(self._ssh, command, ignoreError=True)

        command = "/sbin/vmkload_mod hbr_filter"
        run_ssh(self._ssh, command, ignoreError=True)

        command = "/sbin/vmkload_mod -l"
        filters = run_ssh(self._ssh, command)
        if 'hbr_filter' in filters:
            self.logger.info('Found hbr_filter')
        else:
            self.logger.critical('hbr_filter NOT FOUND')

        self.operationId = generateOpId(index=self.index)

        command = 'logger -t Hostd %s' % self.operationId
        run_ssh(self._ssh, command)

        command = 'logger -t vmkernel %s' % self.operationId
        run_ssh(self._ssh, command)

        self.HostMachine.list()
        try:
            self.logger.debug('remoteStorage.MakeDirectory: %s'
                              % self.testDirectory)
            makeDirResult = self.remoteStorage.\
                MakeDirectory(self.testDirectory)
            self.logger.debug('makeDirResult: %s' % makeDirResult)
        except Vim.Fault.FileAlreadyExists, e:
            self.logger.ignored('Folder already exists: %s\n%s'
                                % (self.testDirectory, str(e)))

        self.datastorePath = str(self.remoteStorage) + ' ' + self.testDirectory
        self.fullPath = pyVim.path.DsPathToFsPath(self.datastorePath)
        self.logger.info('datastorePath:%s' % (self.datastorePath))
        self.logger.info('fullPath:%s' % (self.fullPath))

    @log.low_class_function
    def create(self):
        pass

    def start(self):
        pass

    def stop(self):
        pass

    @log.low_class_function
    def cleanup(self):
        if self.fullPath:
            command = 'rm -fr %s' % self.fullPath
            run_ssh(self._ssh, command, silent=False, ignoreError=False)
        self._ssh.cleanup()

    def waitForTask(self, task, label='', ignoreError=False):
        progress_bar_init(label=label)
        taskResult = None
        try:
            taskResult = pyVim.task.WaitForTask(
                                                task,
                                                raiseOnError=True,
                                                si=self.serviceInstance,
                                                pc=None,
                                                onProgressUpdate=progress_task)
        except Exception, e:
            if ignoreError:
                self.logger.ignored('waitForTask Exception: %s ' % (str(e)))
            else:
                self.logger.critical('waitForTask Exception: %s ' % (str(e)))

        return taskResult

    def ssh(self, command, ignoreError=False):
        (rc, stdout, stderr) = self._ssh.run(command, ignoreError=ignoreError)
        stdout.rstrip()
        if rc == 0:
            if stdout:
                self.logger.remote_output('esx: %s' % (stdout))
            return stdout
        else:
            if ignoreError:
                self.logger.ignored('esx error for: %s' % (stderr))
            else:
                self.logger.error('esx error for: %s' % (stderr))

    def getSourcesPath(self):
        return self.datastore.machineSources

#╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
#║                           L O G G I N G                            ║
#╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
logging: &id_logging
    # do not remove lines, comment out a line to disable
    levels:
      # should be used
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_FOOTER
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_HEADER
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_NOTE
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_TEST
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_TRACE
      # 
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_FUNCTION
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_VERBOSE
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_VVERBOSE
      # optional
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_REMOTE_OUTPUT
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_REMOTE_VITALS
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_REMOTE_LOGS
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_ESX
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_HBR
      - LOGGING_LEVEL_MACHINE
      #- LOGGING_LEVEL_ESX_LOG
      #- LOGGING_LEVEL_HBR_LOG
      #- LOGGING_LEVEL_IGNORED_ERROR
    format: '[%(asctime)s] %(levelname)s [%(module)s:%(funcName)s()@%(lineno)d] %(message)s'
    format_simple: '[%(asctime)s] %(levelname)s %(message)s'
    format_date: '%H:%M:%S'

#╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
#║                         F R A M E W O R K                          ║
#╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
framework: &id_framework
  resultsPath: /tmp/resultsZZZ
  files: files
  logs: logs
  tmp: tmp
  logFile: testcase.log
  tools:
    path: /automation/framework/launch/Tools
    spaz: checkablespaz.py
  vmtree: $VMTREE
  
#╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
#║                            S Y S T E M                             ║
#╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
system: &id_system
  enableNetworkCompression: False
  
#╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
#║                              H  B  R                               ║
#╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
Hbr_credentials: &id_Hbr_credentials 
  user: root
  password: pwd
 
HbrControl: &id_HbrControl
    certificateGenerationScript: fakehms-generate-certificate.sh
    certificateFile: fakehms.crt
    keyFile: fakehms.key

Hbr: &id_Hbr
  Esx: 0
  authentication:
    certificate: 
    key:
    thumbprint: 
  tools:
    ioctl: /sbin/hbrfilterctl
    hbrpsftool: /sbin/hbrpsftool
  filter:
    replicator: hbr_filter
    sample: vscsi_example_filter
  ip: 10.20.109.62
  port: 8123
  credentials: *id_Hbr_credentials
  Machine:
    name: hmsva
  logs:
    - /var/log/messages
    - /var/log/hbrsrv.log
  
#╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
#║                              E  S  X                               ║
#╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
Esx_credentials: &id_Esx_credentials 
  user: root
  password: ca$hc0w
 
datastore: &id_datastore
  name: $DATASTORE
  uuid: $DATASTORE_UUID
  root: 'HBRQA/Gemini'
  machineSources: HBRQA/Gemini/Sources
datastore0: &id_datastore0
  name: $DATASTORE0
  uuid: $DATASTORE_UUID0
  root: 'HBRQA/Gemini'
  machineSources: HBRQA/Gemini/Sources
datastore1: &id_datastore1
  name: $DATASTORE1
  uuid: $DATASTORE_UUID1
  root: 'HBRQA/Gemini'
  machineSources: HBRQA/Gemini/Sources

Esxs_defaults: &id_Esxs_defaults
  tools:
    disk_create: /sbin/diskCreate
    disk_tool: /sbin/diskTool
    hbrpsftool: /sbin/hbrpsftool
  logs:
    - /scratch/log/syslog.log
    - /scratch/log/hostd.log
    - /scratch/log/vobd.log
    - /scratch/log/vmkernel.log
Esxs: &id_Esxs
  - Esx: &id_Esx0
    ip: 10.20.108.246
    datastore: *id_datastore
    credentials: *id_Esx_credentials 
    Hbr: *id_Hbr
#  - Esx: &id_Esx1
#    name: eng_vadpqe_us_1-2015.08.03-12.26.33.esx.0
#    enabled: False
#    ip: 10.161.60.71
#    datastore: *id_datastore0
#    credentials: *id_Esx_credentials
